nodes.name: Mme Ve DOUCHARD-HUZARD
nodes.summary: Mme Ve DOUCHARD-HUZARD was an imprimeur and libraire of the Royal and Central Agricultural Society in Paris, awarded the Grand Gold Medal in 1848

nodes.name: PARIS
nodes.summary: Awarded Grand Gold Medal by Royal and Central Agricultural Society in 1848 for a practical manual on market gardening; Société royale et centrale d'agriculture took interest as early as 1810. Market gardening concentrated within city's surroundings, covering approximately 1,578 hectares; undergoing changes due to railroads and industry.

nodes.name: l'Eperon, Saint-André
nodes.summary: l'Eperon-Saint-André, rue in Paris where Mme Ve DOUCHARD-HUZARD's Imprimerie is located.

nodes.name: Société royale et centrale d'agriculture de la Seine
nodes.summary: Founded a prize in 1843 for the best treatise on market gardening in Paris; awarded grand gold medal of 1,000 francs to J.G. Moreau et J.D. Davenne's work in 1844.

nodes.name: market gardening in Paris
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris has a long history, dating back over 50 years, and has undergone changes due to the construction of railroads and industrial efforts. The practice is encouraged by the Société royale et centrale d'agriculture de la Seine since 1810. A competition for a practical manual on market gardening was opened in 1842, which the authors won with their treatise.

nodes.name: Minister of Agriculture and Trade
nodes.summary: Founded a prize in 1843 for the best treatise on market gardening in Paris

nodes.name: market gardening
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris involves cultivating various crops such as spring white onion, small white onion, red onion, chives, spinach, cabbage, and more. It has been practiced and documented with different land types to provide a valuable source of income for market gardeners.

nodes.name: Market gardeners
nodes.summary: Market gardeners in Paris have a long history dating back fifty to sixty years; they require years of experience and typically start early in August. They are generally receiving only the first elements of instruction and are frightened by the distance between their knowledge and that of literate men.

nodes.name: Royal Agricultural Society
nodes.summary: Founded a prize for best treatise on market gardening in Paris in 1843 and awarded the grand gold medal of 1,000 francs to the authors, published the authors' work at its own expense.

nodes.name: horticulture
nodes.summary: Horticulture in Paris has never been adequately described by non-practitioners. Practising market gardeners are best suited to describe this type of cultivation.

nodes.name: fertilizers
nodes.summary: Fertilizers used by market gardeners in Paris are mostly horse manure and rabbit dung.

nodes.name: amendments
nodes.summary: Improving substances used as fertilizers and amendments.

nodes.name: stimulants
nodes.summary: Improving substances used as fertilizers, amendments and stimulants are discussed in Chapter V of the book on market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: water for irrigation
nodes.summary: Water for irrigation is crucial in market gardening in Paris, but specific details are discussed in Chapter VI.

nodes.name: Water
nodes.summary: Water for irrigation is essential in market gardening in Paris; it's used to destroy insects like flea beetles and for watering plants during dry periods.

nodes.name: Irrigation
nodes.summary: Water for irrigation is a crucial aspect of market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: Tools
nodes.summary: Tools used in market gardening, including tools for irrigation and crop management

nodes.name: Machines
nodes.summary: Tools, instruments and machines are used in market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: Instruments
nodes.summary: Tools and instruments used by Paris market gardeners, including a list of tools such as cultivators, plows, and irrigation machines.

nodes.name: Vegetable-growing operations
nodes.summary: Vegetable-growing operations are discussed in Chapter VIII of a practical manual on market gardening in Paris, covering various aspects such as habits and ways of life of Paris market gardeners and month-by-month market gardening activities.

nodes.name: Paris market gardeners
nodes.summary: Paris market gardeners cultivate vegetables such as cabbage, spinach, onions, lettuce, chives and asparagus; they grow carrots including early season Carrot in second season and Half-long carrot grown only in open ground. They also grow Poméranico conical cabbage, Brunswick stubby cabbage, York cabbage, red onion and tarragon.

nodes.name: Spring white onion
nodes.summary: Spring white onion is grown in Paris market gardens as of 1844.

nodes.name: Small white onion
nodes.summary: Small white onion is grown by market gardeners in Paris around August; same as spring white onion but kept smaller by different culture, sown from February to June and not transplanted

nodes.name: Red onion
nodes.summary: Red onion is grown by Paris market gardeners in sheltered beds in February or March, then transplanted to smoked soil and harvested in September.

nodes.name: Chives
nodes.summary: Chives are a perennial plant in market gardening in Paris, grown from seed or clumps for salads and kitchen gardens.

nodes.name: Spinach
nodes.summary: Spinach is grown by Paris market gardeners in September and August for round-seeded or Dutch spinach and prickly-seeded, common spinach varieties.

nodes.name: Round-seeded spinach
nodes.summary: Round-seeded spinach grows from August 20 to August 30; also known as Holland spinach and Dutch spinach.

nodes.name: Prickly-seeded spinach
nodes.summary: Prickly-seeded spinach is also known as common spinach or English spinach. It was grown by Paris market gardeners in the mid-19th century.

nodes.name: Common spinach
nodes.summary: Common spinach is also known as prickly-seeded spinach. It originates from England.

nodes.name: Cabbage
nodes.summary: Cabbage varieties mentioned in market gardening context: York cabbage; suited for a marsh with northern exposure and also grown on beds. Pests include the guillotine or small white worm that eats the root of cabbage.

nodes.name: York cabbage
nodes.summary: York cabbage is a type of cabbage grown in Paris, including varieties like pain-de-sucre and cœur-de-bœuf, planted after the first frost for winter consumption.

nodes.name: Hâtif en pain de sucre cabbage
nodes.summary: Hâtif en pain de sucre cabbage is a variety of cabbage mentioned in the text as being grown by Paris market gardeners.

nodes.name: Heart of beef cabbage
nodes.summary: Heart of beef cabbage: cultivated in September; related to Pomeranian conical cabbage, Brunswick stubby cabbage, and white cabbage.

nodes.name: Poméranio conical cabbage
nodes.summary: Poméranio conical cabbage is a type of cabbage mentioned in the context of market gardening in Paris in 1844.

nodes.name: Brunswick stubby cabbage
nodes.summary: A type of cabbage from Brunswick.

nodes.name: White cabbage
nodes.summary: White cabbage is grown by market gardeners in Paris and several varieties exist such as Poméranio conical cabbage, Brunswick stubby, and heart of beef.

nodes.name: Red cabbage
nodes.summary: Red cabbage is a type of cabbage grown in Paris, planted for winter consumption, and available in July and September.

nodes.name: Brunswick stubby
nodes.summary: A type of cabbage

nodes.name: large ribbed
nodes.summary: A type of cabbage that is large and ribbed.

nodes.name: Brussels sprouts
nodes.summary: Brussels sprouts are planted for winter consumption.

nodes.name: autumn broccoli
nodes.summary: Autumn broccoli is mentioned as a plant in the text

nodes.name: large salomon
nodes.summary: Large salomon is a type of plant mentioned in the text, possibly a variety of cabbage, which can be harvested from April 25 to May 25.

nodes.name: small salomon
nodes.summary: Small salomon is a type of cauliflower that can be grown in open ground during spring, with harvesting lasting from April 25 to May 25.

nodes.name: spring
nodes.summary: Spring is a time when various vegetables are grown in Paris including small white onion chives and spinach from April to May and red onion in August.

nodes.name: summer
nodes.summary: Summer is a season for growing and harvesting certain crops in the Paris market gardening culture.

nodes.name: Milan curly
nodes.summary: Milan curly is a type of vegetable mentioned in the market gardening chapter.

nodes.name: Cauliflower
nodes.summary: Cauliflower is planted in February and July; hard cauliflowers are sown for autumn harvest, including varieties such as York, pain-de-sucre and cœur-de-bœuf.

nodes.name: white
nodes.summary: White cabbage is mentioned in the text as a type of vegetable grown in Paris, specifically in Chapter X.

nodes.name: violet
nodes.summary: violet is a characteristic of cauliflower (page 125), and a color option for cauliflower

nodes.name: Lamb's lettuce
nodes.summary: Lamb's lettuce is harvested in November and February. Last lamb's lettuce this month along with black lettuce, new radishes, green romaine, sorrel, parsley, chervil.

nodes.name: round
nodes.summary: round-seeded or Dutch spinach

nodes.name: regency
nodes.summary: Regency is a species of lamb's lettuce mentioned in market gardening literature.

nodes.name: Leek or porreau
nodes.summary: Leek or porreau is a plant of the Liliaceae family and garlic genus, with simple roots below and sword-shaped leaves above. It can be cultivated to produce three different types, including leeks eaten with leafy stem, and requires precise sowing methods.

nodes.name: long
nodes.summary: Long is a type of leek, described in the section on vegetables.

nodes.name: autumn
nodes.summary: Autumn is a season that affects the harvest of vegetables in Paris, particularly those grown by market gardeners.

nodes.name: Chervil
nodes.summary: Chervil is mentioned as being sown in July along with escarole, turnips, radishes, and maidenhair.

nodes.name: winter
nodes.summary: Winter is a season in Paris when plants like cauliflower apples and celery for cutting are grown, typically from December to February.

nodes.name: Lettuce
nodes.summary: Lettuce is sold in June as red and grey lettuce, blond and grey romaine.

nodes.name: Romaine chicon
nodes.summary: Romaine chicon is a type of lettuce mentioned as part of the cultivation calendar in the provided text.

nodes.name: Radish
nodes.summary: Radish is mentioned in the context of seasonal vegetables available during a particular month.

nodes.name: Carrots
nodes.summary: Carrots are grown in market gardening and can be sown every ten days in June for winter consumption.

nodes.name: Pea
nodes.summary: Peas are grown through forced cultivation in December and can be sold in bunches of about 40 centimeters in circumference; forcing green asparagus or asparagus with peas is done by growers in the outskirts of Paris.

nodes.name: Melon
nodes.summary: Melons are grown by Paris market gardeners in June and continue to be planted and ripen in August and September; they can be replanted after the trenches are emptied, and the manure is removed from the trenches.

nodes.name: Cantaloupe
nodes.summary: Cantaloupe seeds are oblong, compressed, and leathery-skinned, yellowish-white and smooth; they can be kept for four or five years.

nodes.name: Chicory
nodes.summary: Chicory is sown in July for winter consumption

nodes.name: Italian fine chicory
nodes.summary: Italian fine chicory: half-fine variety

nodes.name: Early white
nodes.summary: Early white is a type of chicory mentioned in the market gardening context.

nodes.name: half-long
nodes.summary: Half-long carrots are a type of carrot mentioned in the chapter on "Land and everything connected with it"

nodes.name: JANUARY
nodes.summary: January is a month when pumpkins can be stored until in a dry, frost-free place.

nodes.name: FEBRUARY
nodes.summary: FEBRUARY is a month when under bell and curly endive are grown in Paris; end of February, cauliflower apples are preserved during the winter.

nodes.name: Gherkin
nodes.summary: Gherkin is one of the vegetables available in abundance at the marshes in September, along with melons, cucumbers, fine and semi-fine chicories, lettuce, and others.

nodes.name: Rouennaise
nodes.summary: Rouennaise cucumber variety

nodes.name: De Meaux
nodes.summary: De Meaux is a type of cucumber mentioned in the chapter on cucumbers and gherkins.

nodes.name: Barbe-de-capucin
nodes.summary: Barbe-de-capucin is a type of vegetable cultivated in Paris. It can be prepared by placing large bunches on a layer of horse manure in a dark cellar, allowing roots to sprout leaves.

nodes.name: Curled-leaf endive
nodes.summary: Curled-leaf endive is grown in the ground for summer consumption.

nodes.name: Beans
nodes.summary: Beans are planted on beds that have been turned over and refurbished, fitted with boxes and frames; beanslageolets are sowed and transplanted at the beginning of the month.

nodes.name: Flageolet beans
nodes.summary: Flageolet beans are a type of bean grown in Paris, discussed in Chapter II, specifically the suivaute species for green eating.

nodes.name: Eggplant or melongene
nodes.summary: Eggplant or melongene: a plant of the solanaceae family and selanum genus, native to the Chands; annual, growing on a stalk about 45cm high.

nodes.name: Tomato or sweet-apple
nodes.summary: Tomato or sweet-apple is grown in Paris and there are two varieties - rouennaise and de Meaux.

nodes.name: asparagus
nodes.summary: Asparagus is planted in February and November. It comes in white and green varieties.

nodes.name: eggplant
nodes.summary: Eggplant seeds are sown on beds in February for later open-ground planting; planted in spring alongside other market garden vegetables and also in August.

nodes.name: melonene
nodes.summary: Eggplant or melongene.

nodes.name: tomato
nodes.summary: Tomatoes are planted in Paris market gardening at the end of August, if not done so in July, and are available in September.

nodes.name: sweet-apple
nodes.summary: Tomato or sweet-apple

nodes.name: chili pepper
nodes.summary: Chili pepper is a plant of the solana family from India introduced to Europe around two hundred years ago; it has several species and varieties but only a few are cultivated by Paris market gardeners.

nodes.name: coral
nodes.summary: Coral is mentioned alongside other vegetables in the market gardening book.

nodes.name: bonne-dame
nodes.summary: Bonne-dame is an annual plant of the Chenopoda family and Arroche genus, native to Asia, growing up to 1 meter high with young triangular leaves edible.

nodes.name: burnet
nodes.summary: Burnet is a perennial plant that can be propagated by bursting and germinates equally well for three years; its seed is brown, oval, wrinkled, ridged and has four wings. It is sown in June and grows as it blooms.

nodes.name: tarragon
nodes.summary: Tarragon is available in May, alongside other plants such as carrots, turnips, and radishes, and also in June.

nodes.name: watercress
nodes.summary: Watercress is a crop sold by market gardeners in Paris, specifically in June, along with other crops like red and grey lettuces, blond and grey romaine.

nodes.name: sorrel
nodes.summary: Sorrel is a seasonal vegetable offered by the marshes in this last month of the horticultural year.

nodes.name: pumpkin
nodes.summary: Pumpkin is planted on beds last month and requires pruning.

nodes.name: scorsonera
nodes.summary: Scorsonera is a plant grown by market gardeners in Paris, occupying the land for 18-20 months and grown using two types of sowing methods: plowing and broadcast or ray sowing. Sown in August, it goes to seed the following summer and can be ready for consumption the following winter after new leaves sprout.

nodes.name: parsnip
nodes.summary: Parsnip is grown in Parisian market gardens and typically harvested and consumed in September.

nodes.name: carde-poirée
nodes.summary: Carde-poirée is a type of greenery mentioned as one of the items sold in May.

nodes.name: cardon
nodes.summary: Cardon is a vegetable that can be grown in Parisian market gardens. To obtain cardoon seed, leave a few plants in place during autumn and cover them over winter.

nodes.name: celery
nodes.summary: Celery is transplanted this month; small celery for cutting can also be directly sown in the ground. First celery for cutting is available.

nodes.name: Turkish Celery with large rib
nodes.summary: Turkish Celery is a type of celery mentioned in a market gardening manual.

nodes.name: Rapunzel
nodes.summary: Rapunzel is a plant grown in June.

nodes.name: Strawberry
nodes.summary: Strawberry is grown by fewer than half a dozen of the eighteen hundred market gardeners in Paris; varieties include Alpine strawberry. Strawberry plants are typically planted 28 centimetres apart in November and covered with boxes and frames to allow them to vegetate during frosts.

nodes.name: Alpine strawberry
nodes.summary: Alpine strawberry is also known as wild strawberry (Fragaria vesca) and mountain strawberry, with varieties including Alpine strawberry. It has a longer fruit-bearing season and tastier fruit compared to other strawberries, making it more marketable for forced cultivation by Parisian market gardeners.

nodes.name: Four-season strawberry
nodes.summary: Four-season strawberry is a type of strawberry mentioned in the context of Paris market gardening in 1848.

nodes.name: Navet tendre des Vertus
nodes.summary: Navet tendre des Vertus: a type of summer turnip, cultivated in July.

nodes.name: Mushroom
nodes.summary: Mushrooms can be harvested in March and are sold together with other vegetables such as carrots, turnips, radishes, sorrel, parsley, certeuil, tarragon, salad burnet, green beans, carde-poirée, and cucumbers in May.

nodes.name: Vegetables
nodes.summary: Vegetables mentioned: white onion, red onion, chives, spinach (round-seeded and prickly-seeded), cabbage (York, hâtif en pain de sucre, heart of beef, Poméranio conical, Brunswick stubby, white cabbage, Red cabbage); grown in Paris, circa 1844. Specific varieties cultivated in August include Spring white onion, hard and semi-hard cauliflower, escarole, coarse and semi-fine chicory, curly savoy cabbage.

nodes.name: Trombe de vent
nodes.summary: Trombe de vent is a term referring to whirlwinds of wind and dust that can cause damage in market gardens.

nodes.name: Vegetable seeds
nodes.summary: Vegetable seeds for market gardening in Paris are harvested and preserved by market gardeners as described in Chapter XII; also used to grow various vegetables such as white onions, small white onions.

nodes.name: Aubergine
nodes.summary: Aubergine seeds are lenticular or kidney-shaped, brick-colored and 2 millimeters in diameter; they are good for two years.

nodes.name: DOUESO D, GOOS DES LEGUMES
nodes.summary: DOUESO D, GOOS DES LEGUMES cultivates vegetables in Parisian market gardening, including white asparagus and aubergine.

nodes.name: White asparagus
nodes.summary: White asparagus is available in March and can be planted outdoors in this month. It may also be preserved for later sale.

nodes.name: Green asparagus
nodes.summary: Green asparagus is available in February; it can be harvested fresh and used for preserves. The last preserved cauliflower and the last white and green asparagus are also harvested this month.

nodes.name: White broccoli
nodes.summary: White broccoli is a plant known to Paris market gardeners

nodes.name: Violet broccoli
nodes.summary: Violet broccoli is mentioned in the chapter on vegetable-growing operations.

nodes.name: Burdock-pear
nodes.summary: Burdock-pear is a plant mentioned in the manual of vegetable growing.

nodes.name: Cardoon
nodes.summary: Cardoon is sown in June, if not already done in a previous month.

nodes.name: Early carrot
nodes.summary: Early carrot - half-long

nodes.name: Half-long carrot
nodes.summary: Half-long carrot is mentioned in the chapter on land and crops, specifically in August for winter sale, to be delivered for consumption the following spring.

nodes.name: Long carrot
nodes.summary: Long carrot is a type of carrot

nodes.name: Large-ribbed celery
nodes.summary: Large-ribbed celery is a biennial plant of the Umbelliferae family. It has fibrous or tuberous roots and large, twice-winged leaves with broad shiny leaflets. The edible parts are petioles, leaves, and in one species, the roots. Cultivation involves sowing seed in shaded ground in May.

nodes.name: Turkish celery
nodes.summary: Turkish celery is a variety of celery that is shorter and stockier than regular celery, with larger ribs and a fuller heart.

nodes.name: Winter chervil
nodes.summary: Winter chervil is a type of chervil cultivated in winter. It can be sown in September and sold from December to February. The cultivation process involves lightly sowing the seed and burying it with a simple harrowing using a rake or fork.

nodes.name: Spring chervil
nodes.summary: Spring chervil: grows in winter and summer seasons, mentioned in the market gardening context.

nodes.name: Summer chervil
nodes.summary: Summer chervil is a variety of chervil that grows back quickly in the wet season and can produce a second crop thirty days after the first.

nodes.name: Italian chicory
nodes.summary: Italian chicory is an annual plant sown in February, on a warm bed under a frame. It's covered with straw mats to germinate quickly.

nodes.name: Semi-fine chicory
nodes.summary: Semi-fine chicory is planted in autumn after removing manure from the trenches and ploughing them.

nodes.name: Rouennaise chicory
nodes.summary: Rouennaise chicory is cultivated by market gardeners in autumn, often planted after melon trenches are emptied and filled with soil.

nodes.name: De Meaux chicory
nodes.summary: De Meaux chicory is a type of chicory which can not be watered without damage with cold water when the sun is shining brightly.

nodes.name: Wild chicory
nodes.summary: Wild chicory seeds are small, oblong, angular and 2 millimeters long, tightly packed in their calyxes.

nodes.name: Crabapple
nodes.summary: Crabapple is a type of plant mentioned in the 'Market Gardening in Paris' chapter, specifically on page 230.

nodes.name: Escarole
nodes.summary: Escarole is planted in autumn cauliflower trenches and requires abundant watering to produce well.

nodes.name: York sprouts
nodes.summary: York sprouts are harvested in September, along with other produce like cabbage and chives.

nodes.name: sweetbread
nodes.summary: sweetbread is mentioned in a list of vegetables and plants that are possibly grown in Paris, including their French names.

nodes.name: beef-heart
nodes.summary: beef-heart is mentioned in August among other crops such as spring white onion and chives

nodes.name: Pomeranian cone
nodes.summary: Pomeranian cone is a type of cabbage, little cultivated in Paris, with excellent quality and conical head.

nodes.name: stubby Brunswick
nodes.summary: Stubby Brunswick is a type of cabbage mentioned in the provided text, specifically listed as '- stubby Brunswick' in the section describing various types of cabbages.

nodes.name: cabus
nodes.summary: Cabus is a type of cabbage, also referred to as white cabbage.

nodes.name: Brussels
nodes.summary: Brussels sprouts; large ribbed; available in June

nodes.name: Solomon
nodes.summary: Solomon is associated with cauliflower varieties

nodes.name: Early white cucumber
nodes.summary: Early white cucumber is mentioned in the context of market gardening practices in Paris.

nodes.name: Alenois watercress
nodes.summary: Alenois watercress is a type of watercress mentioned in the text as part of various plant species.

nodes.name: Mâche commune
nodes.summary: Mâche commune is a type of plant that is also known as regency mache. It has different varieties such as market garden and others.

nodes.name: Cantaloupe melon
nodes.summary: Cantaloupe melon is planted in trenches; other melons are planted after turning over the last empty lettuce and carrot beds.

nodes.name: Turnip
nodes.summary: Turnip seeds can be sown in March and in April and May in strong, cold soils; turnips are planted on beds.

nodes.name: Onion, white
nodes.summary: White onion is mentioned as a crop in Paris market gardening context.

nodes.name: Common sorrel
nodes.summary: Common sorrel is a perennial plant, its leaves and young stems are edible, it has a taproot making it drought-resistant.

nodes.name: Parsley
nodes.summary: Parsley is a seasonal vegetable mentioned in the context of market gardening in Paris, available during August.

nodes.name: Leek, short
nodes.summary: Leek, short; a plant of the Liliaceae family and of the garlic genus, cultivated from September 15th to 20th.

nodes.name: Golden purslane
nodes.summary: Golden purslane is sown in July and included in produce for that month along with other plants such as cauliflower, York cabbage, melons, cucumbers and more.

nodes.name: Romaine green
nodes.summary: Romaine green is a type of lettuce cultivated in Paris during August, under cloches and with specific care and precautions.

nodes.name: Early violet turnip
nodes.summary: Early violet turnip

nodes.name: walls of Paris
nodes.summary: The walls of Paris have been moved back several times, requiring market gardeners to relocate and establish themselves on new soil.

nodes.name: ground
nodes.summary: ground is often prepared for planting and can be used for tarragon cultivation, which is sown as a broadcast crop in April

nodes.name: soil
nodes.summary: Soil preparation for market gardening in Paris involves careful digging and selecting substantial soil; improving substances used as fertilizers include unknown amendments and stimulants; manure is put on the soil where new layers are made to prevent freezing.

nodes.name: Saint-Martin canal
nodes.summary: The Saint-Martin canal was built and a fair number of market gardeners were forced to move further out to make way for the new constructions.

nodes.name: market gardens
nodes.summary: Market gardens in Paris require covering and uncovering plants from frost during winter, and using cloches to protect them during light frosts of spring; escarole seed ripens in September after five or six years.

nodes.name: marais
nodes.summary: Marais refers to market gardens in Paris that were established near marshy areas; the name maraichers is derived from these early gardens.

nodes.name: gardeners
nodes.summary: Market gardeners in Paris traditionally grew vegetables and fruits; to describe market gardening as it was practiced in Paris in 1844, authors J.G. Moreau and J. Davenne composed a practical manual that won the grand gold medal of the Royal and Central Agricultural Society.

nodes.name: frames
nodes.summary: Frames are used to cover layers on which cantaloupe melons and lettuces are planted in large quantities; also for carrots, turnips, radishes and chicory. Stacked in a shed sheltered from the rain when not being used.

nodes.name: Fournier
nodes.summary: Fournier was a market gardener who introduced frames and cantaloupe to market gardening in Paris around 1780.

nodes.name: royal gardens
nodes.summary: Royal gardens in Paris used early produce and stretchers before being adopted by market gardeners, including Fournier who introduced frames and cantaloupe around 1780.

nodes.name: chassis
nodes.summary: A frame or chassis is used in market gardening to support and organize plants. Used for growing black lettuce in layers under the chassis in December, ready for consumption towards end of January.

nodes.name: potatoes
nodes.summary: Potatoes can be double-harvested when tomato grafts perfectly onto the stems of potatoes, producing a harvest in the same space of time and land.

nodes.name: Quentin
nodes.summary: Introduced white asparagus to market gardening in Paris around 1792. Also known for forcing green asparagus around 1800, along with his brother-in-law Marie.

nodes.name: Marie
nodes.summary: Marie was a pioneer in forcing green asparagus around 1800, alongside his brother-in-law Quentin.

nodes.name: Besnard
nodes.summary: Besnard was a market gardener who introduced forced cauliflower cultivation around 1811.

nodes.name: Dulac
nodes.summary: Dulac was one of the first market gardeners to force-crop beans in 1814 and Romaines around 1812.

nodes.name: Chemin
nodes.summary: Chemin was one of the first market gardeners to force Romaines in Paris around 1812.

nodes.name: Baptiste Quentin
nodes.summary: Baptiste Quentin is a market gardener who introduced several new techniques to the field. He and his brother-in-law Marie were the first to force green asparagus around 1800, and he also forced fine Italian chicory.

nodes.name: Fanfan Quentin
nodes.summary: Fanfan Quentin was a market gardener who introduced new processes to the field and was one of the first to force-crop beans in 1814.

nodes.name: Gontier
nodes.summary: Gontier was a market gardener who first used the thermosiphon in forced cultivation under chassis in 1836 and has been using it on his fermented crops for several years.

nodes.name: Baptiste
nodes.summary: Baptiste Quentin forced fine Italian chicory around 1812.

nodes.name: Romaines
nodes.summary: Romaines are planted among cauliflowers in the rows, spaced 66 centimeters apart, and watered to attach them to the soil. They can be sown at the end of October and the beginning of November. If romaines are watered with cold water from wells in hot sun when they're about to be capped or have already been capped, this causes spots of rot on the inside.

nodes.name: Fine Italian chicory
nodes.summary: Fine Italian chicory began to be forced around 1812 by Baptiste Quentin.

nodes.name: colleagues
nodes.summary: Market gardeners in Paris have never written about their profession; they lack time and fear the distance between their knowledge and that of literate men.

nodes.name: marshes
nodes.summary: Marshes in Paris are used by market gardeners for sheltering and stacking bells and frames after they are removed from the layers, as well as preparing manure.

nodes.name: Paris city limits
nodes.summary: The Paris city limits encompass 1,800 market gardens employing around 9,000 people including children and day laborers.

nodes.name: master's children
nodes.summary: The master's children may be used as laborers in a market gardening establishment.

nodes.name: men
nodes.summary: men are part of a market gardener's staff, use watering cans to water plants

nodes.name: garden
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris; includes information on various vegetables such as onions, chives, spinach, and cabbage; also mentions the Royal and Central Agricultural Society's interest in market gardening. Cultivation methods for chicory are described.

nodes.name: seasons
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris occurs year-round but has variations depending on the season and specific month; it requires adapting to changing conditions.

nodes.name: master
nodes.summary: A master market gardener in Paris is typically a male partner who starts with significant expenses for land preparation and setup.

nodes.name: hired girl
nodes.summary: Hired girls are paid according to their strength, intelligence and knowledge; the average wage can be set at 240 fr. per year.

nodes.name: hired boy
nodes.summary: Hired boys are paid by the month, with wages ranging from 18-20 francs per month in winter and 30-32 francs per month in summer, plus 2 francs to 2.5 francs on Sundays. Their pay is not the same as that of hired girls, who receive an average annual wage of 240 francs.

nodes.name: hectare garden
nodes.summary: A hectare garden in Paris requires a staff of five or six people at all times.

nodes.name: Women
nodes.summary: Women are employed in market gardening within the new Paris city limits and can earn around 1 fr. plus food per day.

nodes.name: provinces
nodes.summary: Some provinces provide better workers than others for market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: horses
nodes.summary: 1,300 horses cost from 500 to 800 fr. to buy (avg. 650 fr.). Used for transporting vegetables and manure; some are also used for drawing water via manege or gear pump. Annual cost for 1,300 horses: 1,166,250 fr.

nodes.name: manure
nodes.summary: Manure is used to cover unbound escarole and chicory in November, as well as celery trenches, to protect them from frost; also put on soil for new layers to prevent freezing; mixed with new manure, the beds are replanted with another season of small black lettuce or other vegetables.

nodes.name: wagon
nodes.summary: A wagon is used to transport vegetables and manure in the market gardens of Paris; typically one-horse-drawn, costing between 500-550 francs on average.

nodes.name: manege
nodes.summary: A heavy frame device used to draw water from a well by means of a horse in Paris market gardens.

nodes.name: gear pump
nodes.summary: A device used by Paris market gardeners to draw water from wells for irrigation purposes.

nodes.name: well
nodes.summary: A well is used for irrigation in market gardening in Paris, and market gardeners place barrels in their marshes to receive water from the well via buried pipes or channels.

nodes.name: hoo horses
nodes.summary: Hoo horses are used by market-gardeners for about six months in summer and cost between 150 to 200 fr. or an average of 175 fr. each.

nodes.name: land
nodes.summary: Land preparation for market gardening in Paris involves plowing whenever seeds or plants are to be sown; land owners encourage children's involvement and avoid rainy and frosty conditions that can harm roots. Additionally, mulch is used to cover the beds.

nodes.name: buildings
nodes.summary: Buildings in Paris have led to an increase in land cost for market gardening, causing some gardeners to move away from the city center.

nodes.name: factories
nodes.summary: Factories are mentioned as a factor contributing to the displacement of market gardeners from the center of Paris, due to new buildings and warehouses taking over land.

nodes.name: warehouses
nodes.summary: warehouses are mentioned as part of urban development taking over market gardening land in Paris.

nodes.name: paved road
nodes.summary: A paved road is more valuable than an unpaved lane when it comes to land suitable for market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: unpaved lane
nodes.summary: an unpaved lane may be less valuable than one on a street or paved road due to servitude or poor enclosure

nodes.name: walls
nodes.summary: walls are used for preparing manure in market gardening

nodes.name: soil quality
nodes.summary: Soil quality can be improved by mixing sand and clay; dry soil can be made fertile by moistening it properly, and over-moist soil by drying it out to a certain degree.

nodes.name: francs
nodes.summary: The rent for a hectare of land devoted to market gardening is estimated at 12 to 1,300 francs per hectare, including the gardener's lodging, depending on land quality.

nodes.name: hectare
nodes.summary: A hectare of land devoted to market gardening rents for a variable rate; maintenance costs and profit vary.

nodes.name: glass panels
nodes.summary: There are an estimated 2,160,000 glass panels used in market gardening within the new Paris city limits.

nodes.name: cow manure
nodes.summary: Cow manure is mixed into soil to improve its fertility and used as a fertilizer in the cultivation of various crops.

nodes.name: marshland
nodes.summary: land that has not yet been cultivated and is fertilized from outside using various means

nodes.name: seedlings
nodes.summary: Seedlings are held in water and sprouts in a few days for cardoon; transplanted when seedling has 12 to 15 centimetres (4 to 5 inches) long leaves

nodes.name: mulch
nodes.summary: Mulch is used to cover plots and mark out radii for planting celery in market gardening. A light, short mulch is spread over the whole after sowing rapunzel seed to help it grow.

nodes.name: layer manure
nodes.summary: Layer manure is used to carry compost on layers and boards, as well as to carry away old manure from trenches; it differs from rechaud in that it is made with new manure.

nodes.name: potting soil
nodes.summary: Used in market gardening to mix in paths and make fine potting soil; also used for coating the wheel in goptering process.

nodes.name: fat horse manure
nodes.summary: Used as fertilizer in market-gardening establishments. Contains heat that is harmful to young plants, but loses this heat after a month or so and becomes old manure.

nodes.name: plants
nodes.summary: Plants are grown in market-gardening establishments using various techniques; Specific plants mentioned include chives, spinach, cabbage varieties like Pomeranio conical cabbage.

nodes.name: cucumber
nodes.summary: Cucumbers are part of August's produce in Paris market gardening.

nodes.name: hard soil
nodes.summary: Hard soil is dominated by clay and difficult to work.

nodes.name: sandy soil
nodes.summary: Sandy soil is one of three types of soil used for market gardening in Paris, distinguished by an excess of siliceous sand that destroys necessary cohesion.

nodes.name: clay
nodes.summary: Clay is a type of soil used for market gardening in Paris that can be improved by mixing it with sand.

nodes.name: sand
nodes.summary: Sand is used to improve soil that is too strong or compact by mixing it in a suitable quantity.

nodes.name: artichoke
nodes.summary: Artichoke is protected from frost similar to cardoon: large leaves are cut off and covered with litter.

nodes.name: market hall
nodes.summary: A market hall is preferred to be close to the land to save time and it will be sold or rented at the highest price.

nodes.name: horizontal ground
nodes.summary: A manais placed on horizontal ground or placed on sloping ground

nodes.name: sloping ground
nodes.summary: Sloping ground is a type of local situation in market gardening that can affect the suitability and accessibility of the land.

nodes.name: Cart
nodes.summary: Carts used by market gardeners in Paris are one-horse-drawn and cost from 500 to 550 fr. on average.

nodes.name: Alleyway
nodes.summary: Marshes located on an unpaved alleyway are less valuable due to laborious and time-consuming transport of goods.

nodes.name: Winds
nodes.summary: Violent thunderclaps can kill mushroom kernels and larger mushrooms on millstones outdoors, possibly due to noise, electricity, sudden cooling of the atmosphere, or lightning.

nodes.name: Marsh on level ground
nodes.summary: A marsh on level ground is the most advantageous for market gardening due to less fatigue in cultivation and ease of directing water from sprinklers through pipes without extraordinary expense.

nodes.name: sloping marsh
nodes.summary: A sloping marsh has several disadvantages including water loss and drought during the summer; eastern or southern slopes are recommended for early vegetables unless the soil is clayey.

nodes.name: southern slope
nodes.summary: A southern slope is less desirable for a market garden due to summer heat and drought concerns unless the soil is clayey enough.

nodes.name: clayey soil
nodes.summary: Clayey soil can be suitable for growing summer and part-autumn vegetables on a northern slope.

nodes.name: early vegetables
nodes.summary: Early vegetables are best grown on eastern or southern slopes, except if the soil is clayey and can withstand summer heat and drought. Northern slopes are not suitable for early vegetables but good for summer and part-autumn varieties.

nodes.name: Temperature
nodes.summary: Temperature affects mushroom growth in earthen millstones.

nodes.name: romaine
nodes.summary: Romaine is a crop that can be heated; it gets planted in the open ground in March and can be covered with straw if necessary. It is sold alongside other vegetables in June including red and grey lettuces, blond lettuce, cauliflower.

nodes.name: sun
nodes.summary: The sun's warmth benefits plants through glass; it is used to dry seeds of various plants.

nodes.name: sunrise
nodes.summary: Sunrise exposure has a narrower coastline and receives the same plants or seedlings 2-3 weeks later than noon exposure.

nodes.name: sunset
nodes.summary: sunset exposure has a narrower coastline and receives the same plants or seedlings a fortnight or three weeks later than noon exposure; it can cause burning of plants in east-facing position when sunny after nighttime frost.

nodes.name: east-facing position
nodes.summary: East-facing position has a narrower coastline and receives the same plants or seedlings 2-3 weeks later than noon exposure; can cause burning by sun's rays when freezing at night.

nodes.name: northern exposure
nodes.summary: Northern exposure is a condition that can only be of some use in a marsh during summer for plants that like coolness at this time of year, such as spinach and cabbage. For market gardens with northern exposure, it would be better to have no wall on the north side or a very low one.

nodes.name: pear
nodes.summary: Pear is a plant that can be grown in autumn and be part of the season's supplies along with melons, cucumbers, gherkins, fine chicories, and other seasonal vegetables.

nodes.name: sheds
nodes.summary: Sheds are used by market gardeners to hold equipment, and during winter they are stacked in a shed sheltered from the rain.

nodes.name: sliding boxes
nodes.summary: Sliding boxes are stored in sheds against walls of marshes in Paris, alongside sash panels and doormats.

nodes.name: sash panels
nodes.summary: Sash panels are stored in sheds against walls in marshes when not needed, taking advantage of warmer exposures.

nodes.name: doormats
nodes.summary: Doormats are used to speed up fermentation in the soil preparation process for growing eggplants.

nodes.name: espaliers
nodes.summary: Espaliers are structures used to support vines or fruit trees in a marsh or garden setting, often against walls for added warmth and protection.

nodes.name: rye straw windbreaks
nodes.summary: Rye straw windbreaks are used by market gardeners in Paris to replace walls in marshes.

nodes.name: windbreaks
nodes.summary: Windbreaks are not explicitly mentioned in the provided text for their usual purpose. However, it is mentioned that some people trellis tomatoes against a windbreak, and that the tomato can be grafted onto potato stems to produce two harvests, with one being in the ground.

nodes.name: vegetation
nodes.summary: Vegetation is affected by temperature and requires further care in the 8 to 15 days following pruning.

nodes.name: heat
nodes.summary: used to facilitate and hasten germination by concentrating heat; requires protection from extreme heat to prevent spoilage of the heart

nodes.name: street sweepings
nodes.summary: Street sweepings were once used to improve soil in Paris' marshes but this practice was abandoned by market gardeners within the capital's walls.

nodes.name: melon beds
nodes.summary: Melon beds in trenches provide mulch; debris from demolished manure piles can also be used as mulch.

nodes.name: topsoil
nodes.summary: Topsoil is typically 30cm deep; if soil loses fertility below that depth, it's broken up to 40cm and mixed with cow or horse manure as necessary.

nodes.name: weed
nodes.summary: Weeds are prevented from growing by keeping the square clean, and are removed by ploughing with a pitchfork in March.

nodes.name: horse manure
nodes.summary: Horse manure is accumulated from August to be consumed in November and used for fertilizing, it needs to be stored in piles for a month or six weeks.

nodes.name: mushroom millstones
nodes.summary: Mushroom millstones are a source of mulch.

nodes.name: manure piles
nodes.summary: Manure piles are demolished to make beds and their debris is used to create mulch.

nodes.name: greasy loam
nodes.summary: Greasy loam is a type of potting soil used in market gardening. It is created by decomposing manure from layers on the ground and mixing it with other soil to create a heap.

nodes.name: well water
nodes.summary: Well water is used for watering by vegetable gardeners in Paris; it's clear but hard and cold, often too cold, and may have a little selenite in some areas.

nodes.name: underground pipe
nodes.summary: An underground pipe is used to channel water from a well to half- or three-quarter-buried barrels in marshes for irrigation purposes.

nodes.name: barrels
nodes.summary: Barrels are used in market gardening to receive water from a well; the authors suggest that using basins could be better for watering but note several difficulties stand in their way.

nodes.name: selenite
nodes.summary: Selenite is mentioned as a substance present in well water in some districts of Paris.

nodes.name: florists
nodes.summary: Florists in Paris complain about selenite in well water but market gardeners find it suitable for watering.

nodes.name: river water
nodes.summary: River water is warmer than well water and better for watering when the sun shines warmly.

nodes.name: roots
nodes.summary: Roots of plants are susceptible to damage from pests such as the red-headed chafer and diseases like melting.

nodes.name: carole and chicory
nodes.summary: Carole and chicory are vegetables that can be damaged by cold water when the sun is shining brightly.

nodes.name: watering cans
nodes.summary: Watering cans are used by market gardeners for irrigation to wet the plant when aphids establish themselves on young shoots and to hasten their destruction.

nodes.name: zinc
nodes.summary: Zinc is used to line the bottom of a reservoir that holds water for irrigation.

nodes.name: tin
nodes.summary: Tin is mentioned as a material for watering cans, although market gardeners prefer to use red copper due to its strength and durability.

nodes.name: yellow copper
nodes.summary: Yellow copper is used in making watering cans.

nodes.name: red copper
nodes.summary: Red copper watering cans are heavier and more expensive than those made of zinc, tin, or yellow copper, but stronger and longer-lasting.

nodes.name: knob
nodes.summary: A knob is a feature of watering cans used by market gardeners in Paris for irrigation.

nodes.name: basins
nodes.summary: basins are used for watering in market gardening and a small basin is formed around the base of each eggplant plant

nodes.name: farm
nodes.summary: A farm in Paris uses watering cans for irrigation and spades as plows. Watering cans cost around 30 fr. on average.

nodes.name: copper
nodes.summary: Copper is used for a large one at the bottom of the trough or tank, costing 35 fr.

nodes.name: jugs
nodes.summary: A watering can is used by market gardeners to water their crops; it typically weighs 1 kilogram when empty and holds 10 liters of water.

nodes.name: spade
nodes.summary: A gardening tool used to cultivate land; specifically used for replanting celery and loosening the bottom of trenches.

nodes.name: Binette
nodes.summary: A small, sharp blade with a quarter- or half-circle bent socket, into which a wooden handle 1 to a meter long is inserted. The hoe is used to stir up the soil in plantations where grass is beginning to grow, in order to kill it; it costs 75c.

nodes.name: hoe
nodes.summary: A tool used to lift plants for transplanting by passing the hoe under the roots and weighing them down.

nodes.name: grass
nodes.summary: Grass is killed using a hoe that stirs up the soil in plantations.

nodes.name: planking
nodes.summary: a piece of wooden planking used for edging potting soil

nodes.name: bordoir
nodes.summary: A tool used to edge potting soil in layers; consists of a piece of wooden planking (1m long, 20cm wide) with a small round handle (12cm); costs 75c if bought or 1 fr. 50 c. if made by market gardeners.

nodes.name: kerbstone
nodes.summary: A kerbstone is used in conjunction with a bordoir to edge potting soil in layers and give it consistency and solidity.

nodes.name: layer
nodes.summary: A layer is a portion of soil created and maintained in market gardening; it's isolated by applying manure around its circumference using the torchée method. Layers are used to grow plants such as black lettuce and romaine.

nodes.name: straw
nodes.summary: Straw is used to shelter cauliflower if necessary.

nodes.name: laths
nodes.summary: Laths are used to hold down pea stems at a height of around 16 to 18 centimetres, helping them branch out and become stockier.

nodes.name: plantations
nodes.summary: Plantations are cultivated for tender beans production; the soil sometimes needs to be watered from time to time to prevent drying out and fruit sinking.

nodes.name: loam
nodes.summary: Loam is used until the end of April to prevent soil from drying out and retain nutrients; applied in a layer 12-15mm thick over sown beds or those to be planted. It keeps the soil fresh for melon vegetation.

nodes.name: earth
nodes.summary: Earth is used to coat the wheel for goptering after celery planting; mix in potting soil and apply a layer of very fine, soft earth; white earth shows through the bottom of millstones when mushroom seed is forming; needs contact with them to speed up germination.

nodes.name: rubbish
nodes.summary: Used to transport consumed manure, loam, earth and rubbish; cost around 9 fr.

nodes.name: wooden wedge or cleat
nodes.summary: A piece of wood 3' centimeters thick, 10 centimeters wide and 18 centimeters long used to lift the frames from behind when you want to give air to the plants underneath.

nodes.name: Cinnamon
nodes.summary: A large copper one is needed at the bottom of the trough or tank, costing... 35 fr.

nodes.name: panneau de châssis
nodes.summary: A square piece of oak wood, painted with two coats of oil, measuring 1 meter 33 centimeters on each side and divided into four by three small timbers to support the glass; costs from 10 to 12 fr.

nodes.name: hottriau
nodes.summary: A loader used by market gardeners to load manure onto layers and trenches; costs between 5-6 fr.

nodes.name: frame
nodes.summary: A frame used in gardening to support plants and keep the soil in place; also used for covering beds and trays to protect from frost.

nodes.name: timbers
nodes.summary: Timbers are used to support frames that maintain heat and humidity in market gardening. Two strong pieces of squared timber, about 3 meters high and 22 centimeters square, are planted on opposite sides of a well's curbstone, joined by a crossbeam.

nodes.name: glass
nodes.summary: used in frames to maintain heat and humidity for plant growth

nodes.name: oak wood
nodes.summary: A panel of oak wood, painted and glazed, costs from 10 to 12 fr; average term: 11 fr.

nodes.name: stretcher
nodes.summary: A stretcher is made up of two 2-meter 66-centimeter long arms, joined by four 70- to 80-centimeter long bars, placed in the center, 16 centimeters apart. It is particularly useful for transporting frames from the shed to the trunks and from the trunks to the shed.

nodes.name: cloches
nodes.summary: Cloches are used to protect plants from frost during winter by covering and uncovering them as needed.

nodes.name: fir planks
nodes.summary: Fir planks are used to build chests for market gardening in Paris, preferred over oak due to being less heavy and expensive.

nodes.name: oil paint
nodes.summary: Oil paint is not recommended for chests because it can harm plant roots.

nodes.name: box with three frame panels
nodes.summary: A box with three frame panels costs 6 fr. to 7 fr. 50 c., average term... ... . 7 fr. 25e.

nodes.name: double meter
nodes.summary: A double meter is an essential tool for laying out planks and paths properly in market gardening.

nodes.name: cord
nodes.summary: A cord is essential for laying out planks and paths properly in market gardening; it is sold by weight at the rate of 1fr. per half kilogram.

nodes.name: half kilogram
nodes.summary: A unit of measurement, equivalent to half a kilogram or 1 pound

nodes.name: plank
nodes.summary: A plank is a structural element used in market gardening to hold soil in place and lay out beds for planting, also used to support asparagus boards during forcing.

nodes.name: path
nodes.summary: Path preparation in market gardening involves edging, filling and levelling the soil before planting melons under bells; leaving space between rows of asparagus to prevent spreading, with paths about 2 feet wide; paths need manure to aid growth which once depleted is replaced.

nodes.name: hedge
nodes.summary: A hedge is a wall, windbreak, or protection that shelters vegetables from cold winds.

nodes.name: plein carré
nodes.summary: A plein carré is a type of bed in market gardening, without shelter or protection against cold winds, where vegetables are sown or planted.

nodes.name: toise
nodes.summary: a unit of measurement in market gardening used to measure land for cultivation.

nodes.name: board
nodes.summary: A piece of oak lath used for measuring width of plantings in market gardening; planks are also placed on top of plants to stunt growth and harden them against cold weather.

nodes.name: onion
nodes.summary: Onion varieties mentioned: spring white onion, small white onion, red onion; Sow short, half-long and long carrots, onions, leeks in March

nodes.name: Flail
nodes.summary: A flail is used to beat and break siliqua of turnips and radishes to bring out seeds.

nodes.name: batten
nodes.summary: A batten is attached to a handle by straps and is used with a flail to beat ripe vegetables such as chicory; it costs 1 fr. 50c.

nodes.name: handle
nodes.summary: A handle is a component of various gardening tools such as flail and rake, used to hold the tool and provide leverage.

nodes.name: fork
nodes.summary: A fork is used to rake in and press down seeds such as chives.

nodes.name: bucket
nodes.summary: A bucket is part of a gibet, used in marshes, consisting of three pieces of wood planted in a triangle around a well, 3 meters high, joined at the top by a pulley over which runs a rope with a bucket at each end.

nodes.name: rope
nodes.summary: A rope is used to bind cardoon leaves together at the bottom, middle, and top before wrapping them with litter for blanching.

nodes.name: pulley
nodes.summary: A pulley is a piece of equipment used in market gardening, consisting of two strong pieces of squared timber, about 3 meters high and 22 centimeters square, planted and sealed upright at least a meter apart.

nodes.name: maneges
nodes.summary: Maneges are equipment used in market gardening in Paris. They replaced a device used for shallow wells and are being used more than gear pumps.

nodes.name: hoods
nodes.summary: Hoods are louvered and smaller than ordinary hoods; market gardeners need at least two dozen to artfully arrange vegetables for the market.

nodes.name: hotte
nodes.summary: A hood used in market gardening; made of small wood and wicker; carried on the back by means of two straps; used to carry compost, manure and other loads in a garden or marsh.

nodes.name: compost
nodes.summary: Compost is used in mushroom cultivation, put into the hole left by a picked mushroom and helps produce mushrooms for two or three months.

nodes.name: horse
nodes.summary: used by market gardeners to transport vegetables to the market and bring in manure

nodes.name: crossbeam
nodes.summary: A crossbeam is a piece of wood used in the construction of maneges for wells. It is about 3 meters high and 22 centimeters square.

nodes.name: mannes
nodes.summary: Mannes are baskets without handles used in market-gardening establishments to store vegetables and herbs for transport to the market.

nodes.name: herbs
nodes.summary: Herbs are stored in mannages which cost 90 cents and are used to take various vegetables to the market.

nodes.name: strawberries
nodes.summary: Strawberries are put in a Maniveau for retail sale and become available to consumers as early as March.

nodes.name: mannettes
nodes.summary: Mannettes are wicker baskets used to hold melons and cauliflowers for delivery to the market; they cost 59 to 75 centimes each.

nodes.name: maniveau
nodes.summary: A small wicker chaseret used to put strawberries and mushrooms for retail sale, costing 1 fr. 25 c.

nodes.name: rubble stone
nodes.summary: Rubble stone is a material used for building walls in Paris, specifically for enclosing marshes, at a cost of 10 to 11 francs per running meter.

nodes.name: plaster
nodes.summary: Plaster is used for building walls suitable for enclosing a marsh, at a cost of 10 to 11 francs per running meter in Paris.

nodes.name: diapers
nodes.summary: Diapers are used by market gardeners to cover their beds, bells and frames against cold and hail, and sometimes against the sun.

nodes.name: bells
nodes.summary: Bells are used in market gardening to provide protection and shade for transplanted seedlings.

nodes.name: hail
nodes.summary: Hail is used as a reason for covering diapers, bells and frames in market-gardening establishments.

nodes.name: market-gardening establishments
nodes.summary: Market-gardening establishments in Paris employ children as young as twelve, who learn through experience and help their families by cultivating their own land.

nodes.name: twine
nodes.summary: A gardener's doormat uses twine stretched between posts

nodes.name: stakes
nodes.summary: Stakes are used to mark locations and guide manure distribution in market gardening, typically placed at the four corners of a square with strings stretched between them.

nodes.name: string
nodes.summary: A string is used to tie around cauliflower apples, typically by tying it to the core of each apple and hanging them upside down from nails on floor joists.

nodes.name: loom
nodes.summary: A device used to sew a doormat.

nodes.name: wood
nodes.summary: Wood is used in frames for planting lettuce and cucumbers; and may be present in manure piles along with hay and litter.

nodes.name: rye
nodes.summary: Rye straw is used for sewing.

nodes.name: bobbins
nodes.summary: Used in sewing a doormat

nodes.name: spool
nodes.summary: A spool is used in sewing a doormat and costs around 50 cents for a mat 2 meters long and 1 meter 40 centimeters high.

nodes.name: English shovel
nodes.summary: An English shovel is a tool that costs from 6 to 8 francs; the average price being 7fr.

nodes.name: Wooden planter
nodes.summary: A wooden planter is a round piece of wood used to plant lettuces, onions, and other plants. It has a blunt point at the bottom and is curved like a corbin beak at the top.

nodes.name: Iron planter
nodes.summary: Iron planters are used to plant cabbages in rows, spacing them 33 centimeters apart.

nodes.name: foundation
nodes.summary: A foundation for a well in Paris market gardening can cost between 90 to 100 fr. per meter on average.

nodes.name: subsoil
nodes.summary: The subsoil can be of a poor nature and when de-sinking, it may render the land sterile for some time by bringing bad soil to the surface. However, market gardeners never expose themselves to this inconvenience as their land must yield immediately.

nodes.name: iron teeth
nodes.summary: Iron teeth are part of a rake, used to scrape and smooth soil, and cost between 2-2.50 francs.

nodes.name: Rake
nodes.summary: A tool used for breaking up small clods and to rake or cut by the root weeds growing in paths or hard areas; also used to bury chervil seed with a simple harrowing after sowing, level out the entire surface of plots passed over it.

nodes.name: Push rake
nodes.summary: A push rake is used for rapid weeding in large plantations and stirring the soil to a depth of 1 or 2 centimeters.

nodes.name: Pull rake
nodes.summary: A pull rake is a type of equipment used for raking paths and hard spots in large plantations.

nodes.name: Serfouette
nodes.summary: A tool used in market gardening for crutching the earth in plantations and preparing it to receive watering. It has a blade or two teeth and costs 75c.

nodes.name: Single serfouettes
nodes.summary: Single serfouettes have a small, sharp blade, and two teeth.

nodes.name: Scythe iron
nodes.summary: Scythe iron is a type of metal used for making farm tools and machines. It was mentioned as a material for push rakes and pull rakes.

nodes.name: blade
nodes.summary: A blade is a part of a tool used for crutching the earth in plantations.

nodes.name: wine barrels
nodes.summary: Wine barrels are used by market gardeners in their marshes to receive water from wells through buried pipes, but they only last three or four years and cost around 5 or 6 francs each; oil barrels are preferred nowadays due to higher capacity and longer lifespan.

nodes.name: wicker
nodes.summary: Wicker is used to bind wine barrels in market gardening; these barrels cost 5-6 fr each but only last 3-4 years.

nodes.name: pipes
nodes.summary: Pipes are used in market gardening to receive water from a well by means of buried channels on the ground.

nodes.name: channels
nodes.summary: Channels are used to bring water from a well to barrels for irrigation.

nodes.name: oil pipes
nodes.summary: Oil pipes are preferred over wine barrels due to their larger size and iron hoops. They can contain around 540 liters of water.

nodes.name: iron hoops
nodes.summary: Iron hoops are preferred for oil barrels as they make the wood resistant to rotting for 12-15 years.

nodes.name: reservoir
nodes.summary: A small reservoir is used to hold water drawn from the well by a manege or gear pump. The bottom of this reservoir is usually lined with lead or zinc and pierced with a hole for a piece of lead pipe.

nodes.name: lead
nodes.summary: Lead is used to line the bottom of reservoirs and for pipes.

nodes.name: pipe
nodes.summary: Preferred for irrigation due to their size and durability; contain around 540 liters of water

nodes.name: copper cinnamon
nodes.summary: A copper cinnamon is a piece of lead pipe divided in two by a large copper cinnamon used to fill and close barrels.

nodes.name: cast iron
nodes.summary: Cast iron is a type of pipe that can be used by market gardeners, but it's not their preferred choice.

nodes.name: sandstone
nodes.summary: Sandstone is a preferred material for pipes in market gardening. It's made of pieces of sandstone pipe fitted end-to-end and puttied with fountain putty, allowing multiple branches to be established on the main pipe.

nodes.name: fountain putty
nodes.summary: Fountain putty is used to seal sandstone pipes that carry water from lead pipes in market gardening irrigation systems.

nodes.name: stoneware
nodes.summary: Stoneware is mentioned as a material for pipes in the context of market gardening.

nodes.name: stoneware pipes
nodes.summary: Stoneware pipes have internal diameters of 5 and a half centimeters (costing 68c per meter) or 8 centimeters (costing 75c per meter).

nodes.name: basketwork
nodes.summary: Basketwork is used for winnowing and cleaning seeds in market gardening operations.

nodes.name: seeds
nodes.summary: Seeds are obtained from various plants in market gardening; chives seeds are described in Chapter VIII.

nodes.name: accot
nodes.summary: Accot is a good material used to surround boxes and protect them from frost in winter.

nodes.name: ados
nodes.summary: ados is a structure for planting seedlings under three different conditions: in October the seed should emerge in three or four days and care should be taken to ensure that the sun does not tire the young plant under the cloches; ados can also serve as a location for transplanting blond romaine throughout winter, including lettuce.

nodes.name: head lettuce
nodes.summary: Head lettuce is supplied to Paris markets from January by market gardeners.

nodes.name: north
nodes.summary: The north wind is a factor in training ados in our marshes.

nodes.name: south
nodes.summary: preferably placed at a certain distance in front of a north-facing wall to train ados

nodes.name: strip of land
nodes.summary: A strip of land used for making ados in market gardening, with a slope to the south and a width indicated above.

nodes.name: clods
nodes.summary: Clods are used for planting seedlings; their size can be comparable to a 5-franc coin.

nodes.name: shovel
nodes.summary: used to press down potting soil and to beat the manure mound for temperature control

nodes.name: vegetable
nodes.summary: Vegetables need to grow fast and well; a properly watered vegetable retains its tenderness, develops fully and looks healthy; specific vegetables mentioned include spring white onion, small white onion, red onion, chives, spinach, round-seeded or Dutch spinach, prickly-seeded, common spinach or English spinach

nodes.name: ploughing
nodes.summary: Ploughing is an essential operation in market gardening as part of various processes like tilling, bedding, and edging.

nodes.name: frost
nodes.summary: Frost affects plants by killing them as they approach; during August market gardeners continue to protect from frost.

nodes.name: night
nodes.summary: Night is mentioned as a time when actions are taken to protect lettuce from frost; mats are used to cover the bells at night, and manure is placed between the bells for further protection if needed.

nodes.name: apple
nodes.summary: Apple is not mentioned in the text; however, it might be referred to as a part of cauliflower, such as 'the best apples' on cauliflowers sown in September to collect seeds ten or eleven months later.

nodes.name: mouth
nodes.summary: Used for watering certain large vegetables such as cauliflower and cardoons.

nodes.name: hoeing
nodes.summary: Hoeing is a practice used to remove weeds and prepare soil by stirring the top layer to a depth of 6-10 centimetres.

nodes.name: Border
nodes.summary: A border is a vertical wall of soil or potting mix around the surface of a bed in market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: bed
nodes.summary: A bed in market gardening is a raised plot of land used for planting seeds such as lettuces, onions, and cabbages; also used for planting beanslageolets, beans, melons, and cucumbers in frames; requires moderate and judicious watering in February to guard against late frosts

nodes.name: box
nodes.summary: Boxes are used in market gardening to cover strawberry beds and boxes and frames, to continue vegetating the plants during frosts.

nodes.name: potting mix
nodes.summary: used to solidify and raise soil or potting mix on top of a bed, 12 to 16 centimetres high

nodes.name: atmospheric influences
nodes.summary: Influences the growth of plants through temperature and humidity and is an important factor in breaking up soil to make it more permeable to air, heat, and watering.

nodes.name: planter
nodes.summary: A planter is used in market gardening to plant green romaine and semi-hard cauliflowers at specific spacings.

nodes.name: leaves
nodes.summary: Leaves are part of a plant in market gardening; clean and remove any damaged leaves from lettuces, romaine and other plants.

nodes.name: chard
nodes.summary: Chard is a cultivated variety of beet, its edible parts being the radial leaves; it goes to seed in June and is replaced by new seedlings.

nodes.name: tubers
nodes.summary: Tubers are formed by ridging potatoes to increase the number of tubers.

nodes.name: potting compost
nodes.summary: a soil or potting compost necessary for the growth of Alpine strawberries.

nodes.name: cold
nodes.summary: Cold can damage plants and affect their growth; frost protection is important.

nodes.name: clochee
nodes.summary: A clochee is a type of frame used in market gardening to protect plants from frost; not essential but helps young plants take root.

nodes.name: heart of the plant
nodes.summary: the heart of the plant is hidden when romaine leaves fold inwards to form a cap

nodes.name: rows
nodes.summary: Rows of celery are covered with earth, and then uncovered two times (empiéter and rechausser) to blanch the stalks.

nodes.name: coftres
nodes.summary: coffres are frames filled with potting soil used to plant lettuce, carrots, radishes, and cantaloupes

nodes.name: crepe lettuces
nodes.summary: Crepe lettuces are a type of lettuce that can be planted under a bell or cold frame and in the open ground.

nodes.name: small black lettuces
nodes.summary: Small black lettuces can be replanted after the old beds are turned over and mixed with new manure.

nodes.name: cantaloupes
nodes.summary: Cantaloupes are sown at the beginning of May, transplanted around the 10th or 12th, and planted in place towards the end of the month; or a mother layer is made to sow cantaloupes at the beginning of February.

nodes.name: winter layers
nodes.summary: Winter layers are used for forced cultivation under cloches in January, with three rows of cloches placed on top of a 12-13 cm thick layer of warmed-up potting soil

nodes.name: spring layer
nodes.summary: 10-12 cm compost layer for chicory growing

nodes.name: trench layer
nodes.summary: A type of layer useful for growing second-season cantaloupe melons under a frame; first trench is 1m wide and 33cm deep, soil carried to the last trench location, with layers built up to 66cm high. Typically made in spring when danger of frost lessens.

nodes.name: melon plant
nodes.summary: Melon plants can be harmed by steam from whole horse manure but not gelding manure; young melon plants can be killed by this steam; plant them under bells and water immediately to protect from sun until they pick up. The first pruning of the young melon plant is typically performed by cutting it about 12 millimeters (6 lines) above the second leaf, with some gardeners removing the cotyledons at the same time.

nodes.name: trench bed
nodes.summary: A trench bed is a type of cultivation method used by market gardeners in Paris, involving the creation of multiple layers in trenches for growing cantaloupes and other crops; instead of a trench bed, cucumbers can be grown in round or square holes filled with well-pressed manure and soil.

nodes.name: panels
nodes.summary: Panels are used for planting melons in trenches and covered with frames; care must be taken to ensure that each vegetable does not lack the moisture it needs.

nodes.name: thermometer
nodes.summary: A thermometer is used by market gardeners to monitor temperature.

nodes.name: melon seedlings
nodes.summary: Melon seedlings are transplanted to a nursery bed and topped five or six days before planting. They are planted in trenches exposed to the sun, directed from east to west.

nodes.name: steam
nodes.summary: Steam can escape from hot manure and is harmful to young plants. The manure of geldings does not produce the same effect.

nodes.name: whole horse manure
nodes.summary: Whole horse manure is hotter than barrack manure, and steam escaping from it can sometimes kill young melon seedlings.

nodes.name: barrack manure
nodes.summary: Barrack manure is less hot than inn manure and geldings' manure; it loses its fire when wet and mixed with new manure to make layers.

nodes.name: geldings manure
nodes.summary: Geldings manure is less hot than whole horse manure. Whole horse manure can sometimes kill young melon seedlings under chassis when concentrated or retained; old manure loses heat and takes on a different title after one month.

nodes.name: June
nodes.summary: June is a month when market gardeners can grow lettuce and sow pink radishes for seed production.

nodes.name: Garlic
nodes.summary: A plant of the Liliaceae family and of the garlic genus

nodes.name: tuff
nodes.summary: a hard, compact soil type that may be present in subsoil; when encountered in market gardening, tuff is not broken up if the topsoil layer is shallow

nodes.name: trench
nodes.summary: A trench is dug facing north, at least 66 cm wide and deep for spawn preparation, filled with manure and soil, used in market gardening to plant melons.

nodes.name: double metre
nodes.summary: A double metre is used to fix the width of a bed in market gardening and to set out planks. It is typically 2 meters 35 centimetres (7 feet) long.

nodes.name: chalking line
nodes.summary: A tool used to mark out paths along either side of the plank, each path must be 33 centimeters (1 foot) wide.

nodes.name: room
nodes.summary: a space where soil is filled and trenches are dug for vegetable gardening purposes

nodes.name: harrow
nodes.summary: a tool used by market gardeners for breaking up large clods and bringing small ones back onto the path for watering purposes

nodes.name: female melon flower
nodes.summary: female melon flower is called a carlle; in market gardening, only melons are topped when they have two leaves

nodes.name: male flower
nodes.summary: Male flowers of sorrel are grown in gardens and do not produce seeds.

nodes.name: Rabbit dung
nodes.summary: Rabbit dung is a fertilizer used by Paris market gardeners when they have any; it's buried sparingly in kind and used to make layers and mulch after being used.

nodes.name: Romaine coast
nodes.summary: Romaine coast is a type of plant that can be interplanted with cauliflowers and needs two to three rows left empty for this purpose.

nodes.name: litter
nodes.summary: Litter is used to protect plants from frost and freezing temperatures.

nodes.name: ceruse
nodes.summary: Ceruse is used to mend broken bell pieces in a specific method.

nodes.name: market gardeners of Paris
nodes.summary: Market gardeners of Paris have been perfecting their craft over the past fifty to sixty years and have developed expertise in vegetable growing and market gardening.

nodes.name: young plants
nodes.summary: Young plants can be left to overwinter in the bed where they were sown and transplanted after severe frosts have passed.

nodes.name: gauge
nodes.summary: A gauge is a type of ditch in market gardening, specifically in ploughing, used to divide the soil properly.

nodes.name: ditch
nodes.summary: a ditch in market gardening is a gauge, a kind of ditch the ploughman must always have in front of him between the ploughed land and that which has not yet been ploughed.

nodes.name: plough
nodes.summary: A tool used by market gardeners in Paris to prepare the soil for sowing or planting. Ploughing should be done when the soil is not too wet or frozen, ideally in well-sheltered spots facing south.

nodes.name: meules
nodes.summary: large heaps of manure used by market gardeners in Paris

nodes.name: millstone
nodes.summary: A millstone is used to process mushroom spawn for growing mushrooms in market gardening; it can be affected by thunder, which kills the fungus grain of shirt-clad millstones on the ground; those made in cellars are not affected.

nodes.name: manure forks
nodes.summary: Manure forks are folded in half, with their backs placed on the edge of the layer.

nodes.name: terne
nodes.summary: Terne is a material used in the context of market gardening in Paris, mentioned alongside loam as something that layers are loaded with.

nodes.name: frame panels
nodes.summary: Frame panels are used to cover layers of lettuce; 30 black lettuces per frame panel, and also for growing carrots under frame in early seasons. Additionally, they can hold up to two hundred transplanted plants.

nodes.name: hanger
nodes.summary: a hanger is used to stack frame panels away from the rain when they are no longer needed

nodes.name: hand
nodes.summary: used for transplanting young plants under cloches and plumping the soil

nodes.name: lettuces
nodes.summary: Lettuces are cleaned and damaged leaves removed after the frost; lettuces such as Romaine require air and need protection from excess humidity.

nodes.name: Cabbages
nodes.summary: Cabbages are cultivated in Paris market gardens. Varieties include Poméranio conical cabbage and Brunswick stubby.

nodes.name: inner leaves
nodes.summary: The inner leaves of cabbage and lettuce plants press strongly against each other and form a compact head when they 'pomment'.

nodes.name: head
nodes.summary: a compact mass of white, fleshy, tender granules; bits of leaf protect the sides from knocks and pressure

nodes.name: rechaud
nodes.summary: A rechaud is a layer of new manure in the paths used to heat and warm up the surrounding area in market gardening.

nodes.name: new manure
nodes.summary: New manure is applied after planting melons and replenished as needed to fill paths in melon gardens.

nodes.name: teens
nodes.summary: A gardening tool used for replanting and providing space, typically done in early June.

nodes.name: November
nodes.summary: November is a month when cardoons are successively blanched in Paris until November.

nodes.name: December
nodes.summary: December is a month for planting and selling lettuces in Paris. It marks the start of the season when black or crepe lettuces are planted on beds under frames, ready to be picked and sold by January.

nodes.name: weeder
nodes.summary: A small snake-shaped instrument used for weeding.

nodes.name: manure dumps
nodes.summary: Manure dumps are necessary in market gardening to utilize space.

nodes.name: bulge at the base of an onion
nodes.summary: The bulge at the base of an onion is a natural process called 'twist'. Heavy rains can prevent this process and cause the onion to remain on the cob.

nodes.name: cob
nodes.summary: a part of a plant

nodes.name: rain
nodes.summary: No information about rain was mentioned in this text.

nodes.name: tracers
nodes.summary: Tracers refer to lines made along the length of a bed to sow or plant vegetables. Market gardeners in Paris draw these lines by walking regularly with their feet apart.

nodes.name: sticks
nodes.summary: Sticks are not mentioned in the context of being used for market gardening practices; however, Paris market gardeners draw lines along beds using their feet instead.

nodes.name: sons of a market gardener
nodes.summary: become accustomed to market gardening work under their father's example and wishes

nodes.name: market gardener's daughters
nodes.summary: Market gardener's daughters rarely marry outside their father's profession and typically become accustomed to the work under their father's example.

nodes.name: Providence
nodes.summary: Providence is mentioned as a source of confidence for market gardeners in Paris, giving them tranquility and harmony.

nodes.name: carriage
nodes.summary: A horse and carriage used by market gardeners to transport vegetables to the market and bring in manure.

nodes.name: bells and frames
nodes.summary: used by market gardeners in Paris to protect plants from frost; used with straw mats for insulation; part of a larger system that includes other structures like hotbeds and bell-glasses; may be covered or uncovered depending on the temperature.

nodes.name: Paris marshes
nodes.summary: The Paris marshes are used for market gardening and early vegetable cultivation, including the growth of cardoons.

nodes.name: mistress
nodes.summary: The mistress of a market garden is involved in hard work including preparing and arranging vegetables for sale, setting off to the market at two or four o'clock in the morning depending on the season and handling all the money from sales throughout the year.

nodes.name: oerfeil
nodes.summary: Oerfeil is a type of plant, often associated with women's work in market gardening.

nodes.name: girl
nodes.summary: A girl helps the mistress of a market gardener's household by accompanying her to the market with their cart of goods.

nodes.name: boy
nodes.summary: helps mistress in delivering vegetables to market

nodes.name: market-gardener class
nodes.summary: Market gardeners in Paris have a long history and are part of a class that is known for its simplicity, purity of morals, and desire to do business honorably.

nodes.name: crops
