nodes.summary: Market gardeners in Paris cultivate various crops including white onion, red onion, chives, spinach, cabbage and others. Crops are water-loving and require support in the wet, such as carrots which are sown every month until August.

nodes.name: savings bank
nodes.summary: Market gardeners' children start working and saving at a young age. At twelve, they receive land to cultivate their own crops. They learn from experience and are encouraged by their fathers to not spend unnecessarily, often starting savings accounts as young as thirteen, fifteen.

nodes.name: Saint Fiacre
nodes.summary: Saint Fiacre is a patron saint of market gardeners in Paris.

nodes.name: flowers
nodes.summary: Small, numerous, clustered, very small, insignificant flowers of the pear plant are followed by small, kidney-shaped seeds.

nodes.name: market
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris; history over 50-60 years; procedures, aims and results published by market gardeners J.G. Moreau and J. Davenne

nodes.name: church
nodes.summary: A church is decorated and adorned by brotherhoods for a fee as part of a ceremony that includes a mass and heavenly blessings for gardeners.

nodes.name: family
nodes.summary: Market gardeners in Paris often have to work into old age and are accustomed to living by work alone; they may offer their services to more fortunate colleagues for a small wage.

nodes.name: vegetable-growing
nodes.summary: Vegetable-growing in Paris has unique procedures and results; the Société royale et centrale d'agriculture de la Seine promoted knowledge of market gardening through a competition and published a practical manual. The authors shared with the Royal Agricultural Society the desire that a practical manual of vegetable growing in Paris should be produced and published.

nodes.name: fresh-growing
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris, including cultivation methods for specific vegetables and crops like cabbage.

nodes.name: peaches
nodes.summary: not grown by Paris market gardeners due to high land cost and uncertain sale

nodes.name: baselle
nodes.summary: Baselle is a type of vegetable not commonly grown in Paris due to its low demand and expensive land costs.

nodes.name: hemisphere
nodes.summary: Northern hemisphere

nodes.name: August
nodes.summary: August is a month in Market gardening in Paris where spring white onion, small white onion, chives, red onion and spinach are cultivated.

nodes.name: tilling
nodes.summary: Tilling is a process in market gardening that involves breaking and turning over the soil to prepare it for planting.

nodes.name: edging
nodes.summary: Edging is a practice in market gardening that involves creating and maintaining edges around plants or gardens.

nodes.name: earthworms
nodes.summary: Earthworms can harm curly endive by being its enemy, also known as the cutworm, or large gray caterpillar.

nodes.name: white onion
nodes.summary: White onion is planted in August and available from May to November.

nodes.name: false thaws
nodes.summary: False thaws can damage York cabbage and other plants, despite its cold hardiness.

nodes.name: hen's egg
nodes.summary: a size comparison for onions

nodes.name: light, dry soils
nodes.summary: Onions require frequent watering in light, dry soils when weather turns dry.

nodes.name: weather
nodes.summary: Weather conditions affect vegetable growth; dry weather requires more frequent watering; sunny and mild weather allows for airing frames.

nodes.name: vegetable gardens
nodes.summary: Vegetable gardens are used for cultivating various vegetables and utilize techniques like rechaud manure placement in paths and accot manure around layers.

nodes.name: onion leaf
nodes.summary: Onion leaves are not mentioned in this text; however the text mentions that Small white onion, Red onion and Chives can be grown.

nodes.name: smoked soil
nodes.summary: Used for red onion growth; onions are pricked out into this type of soil in April and May, spaced 15-16 centimetres apart.

nodes.name: sheltered bed
nodes.summary: A sheltered bed is used to overwinter Passion lettuce. It can also be used for growing Romaine and Chicon.

nodes.name: hard path
nodes.summary: A hard path is used to dry red onions if it rains.

nodes.name: greenhouse
nodes.summary: used to protect cardoons from frost and store uprooted escarole and chicory during winter

nodes.name: spring onion
nodes.summary: Spring onions are sown from late February to late March and sold in June.

nodes.name: cloves
nodes.summary: Chives are grown by market gardeners of Paris and kitchen gardens for their cloves, which clump together in the spring.

nodes.name: Chenopoda
nodes.summary: Chenopoda is a plant family that includes annual plants like the Bonne-Dame; also contains purslane and pear, which are cultivated for their edible leaves.

nodes.name: spinach leaves
nodes.summary: Spinach leaves are annual, 30 to 60 cm high, twining, with simple hastate leaves; flowers small, dioecious, greenish. Fruits from hardened calyx contain a single seed.

nodes.name: HOLLAND SPINACH
nodes.summary: Holland spinach is a type of round-seeded spinach sown from August 20 to August 30 and should not be sown too thickly.

nodes.name: mulch of very short manure
nodes.summary: a light mulch of very short manure is spread over the sowing area to prevent harming the seed when it rises

nodes.name: knife
nodes.summary: A knife is used to cut off leaves of spinach 3 centimeters from the ground for the first harvest.

nodes.name: SPINACH WITH PRICKLY SEED
nodes.summary: Spinach with prickly seed is sown from February to May. It requires moderate temperature and humid conditions. Two harvests are possible from seedlings sown in May. The leaves of this type of spinach are broader and less likely to yellow

nodes.name: COMMON OR ANGLE SPINACH
nodes.summary: Common or Angle Spinach is a type of spinach that can be sown from February to May and tolerates hot weather better than the round-seeded variety.

nodes.name: May
nodes.summary: May is a month when the plant goes to seed and ends its productive cycle

nodes.name: CHOU
nodes.summary: CHOU is a plant of the cruciferous family and the genus whose name it bears.

nodes.name: cruciferous family
nodes.summary: a plant family that includes cabbage and chou, contains species such as little black lettuce, horseradish genus, and varieties like Rave and radish

nodes.name: SEPTEMBER
nodes.summary: September is a month for sowing cabbage varieties including York cabbage, hâtif en pain de sucre cabbage, heart of beef cabbage, Poméranio conical cabbage and Brunswick stubby cabbage.

nodes.name: stems
nodes.summary: The seeds of curly endive and wild chicory, remain on their stems for several years without falling off

nodes.name: glaucous cabbages
nodes.summary: Glaucous cabbages are biennial plants from the cruciferous family, with stems rising up to 2 metres and leaves forming a compact head. They are typically grown by market gardeners in Paris for human consumption.

nodes.name: feet
nodes.summary: used to press down a centimetre-thick bed of potting soil after sowing carrot seeds

nodes.name: rays
nodes.summary: 6 to 8 centimeters deep in planks 2 meters 33 centimeters wide for shelter during winter

nodes.name: west
nodes.summary: Located in relation to 'east' in context of York cabbage nursery; mentioned as a direction for tracing rays to shelter cabbages during winter.

nodes.name: basket
nodes.summary: a container used to carry lifted York cabbages from the nursery before planting them in the rays

nodes.name: snow and ice
nodes.summary: accumulate in dimples near cabbage plants, melt when facing south

nodes.name: SUGARLOAF CABBAGE
nodes.summary: SUGARLOAF CABBAGE is a type of cabbage sown in the first days of September and transplanted into the nursery in October.

nodes.name: COEUR-DE-ROEUF CABBAGE
nodes.summary: COEUR-DE-ROEUF CABBAGE: a type of cabbage with a larger and longer head than York cabbage, sown at the same time as other varieties but planted with six rows in a bed 2 meters 53 centimeters wide.

nodes.name: Pomeranian cone cabbage
nodes.summary: Pomeranian cone cabbage is a type of plant that is typically sown in spring from late March to early May and can be harvested in September or November.

nodes.name: beef cabbages
nodes.summary: Beef cabbages are planted in rows at a distance of 48 centimeters and require ordinary care. They can be picked in September and become very hard if left to grow until November. Heart of beef cabbage is mentioned as an item for autumn, alongside other vegetables.

nodes.name: Stubby Brunswick Cabbage
nodes.summary: Stubby Brunswick Cabbage is a cabbage variety grown by market gardeners farthest from Paris' center, recognizable by its short stalk and medium-sized, flattened head.

nodes.name: March
nodes.summary: March is a month when watercress sowing begins

nodes.name: Brunswick cabbage
nodes.summary: Brunswick cabbage is a type of cabbage grown by market gardeners in Paris. It has a short stalk and a medium-sized, flattened head that appears to rest on the ground. The species is robust, endures cold, and is only eaten in winter.

nodes.name: stalk
nodes.summary: A plant part that emerges in spring from lamb's lettuce plants, and is used for celery growth; also can be attacked by criocer, a red insect that eats the bark of stalks.

nodes.name: CABBAGE WITH LARGE RIBS
nodes.summary: CABBAGE WITH LARGE RIBS is grown in a circumscribed way like white cabbage and has large, full, glaucous leaves with whitish ribs; it is sown in spring and eaten in late autumn and winter.

nodes.name: flea beetles
nodes.summary: Flea beetles are a pest that can devour crucifer seedlings, especially those of turnip and cabbage in dry springs; frequent watering helps to keep them off.

nodes.name: apples
nodes.summary: Apples are mentioned in relation to the size of melons, comparing them to apples or a fist in terms of size.

nodes.name: savoy cabbage
nodes.summary: Savoy cabbage is planted in June; it's of the same blackish color as cauliflower and seeds are good for eight or nine years.

nodes.name: green cabbage
nodes.summary: Green cabbage is a type of savoy cabbage, grown mainly by market gardeners in the Vaugirard, Grenelle and Gros-Caillou districts. It can be recognized by the height of the stalk, size and embossing of leaves, and head shape.

nodes.name: Vaugirard
nodes.summary: Vaugirard is a district in Paris where market gardeners cultivate savoy cabbage.

nodes.name: Grenelle
nodes.summary: Grenelle is a district in Paris where market gardeners grow savoy cabbage.

nodes.name: Gros-Caillou
nodes.summary: Gros-Caillou is one of the districts where market gardeners grow savoy cabbage.

nodes.name: Vaugirard district
nodes.summary: Savoy cabbage is grown by market gardeners in this district

nodes.name: Grenelle district
nodes.summary: Market gardeners in the Grenelle district grow Savoy cabbage, planting two rows per bed and watering sparingly to prevent fast growth.

nodes.name: Gros-Caillou district
nodes.summary: Gros-Caillou district is one of the districts in Paris where Savoy cabbage is grown by market gardeners.

nodes.name: April
nodes.summary: April is a month when curly endive and its varieties are planted out.

nodes.name: July
nodes.summary: July is a month when raiponce is sown, and seeds ripen in July.

nodes.name: curly cabbage
nodes.summary: Curly cabbage degenerates easily and has a musky taste

nodes.name: Curly Savoy cabbage
nodes.summary: Curly Savoy cabbage is planted in August for winter sales and is also sown in another season.

nodes.name: CHOU-FLEUR
nodes.summary: CHOU-FLEUR is a species of cabbage that forms its head from flowers rather than leaves, resulting in a compact mass of white, fleshy, tender granules. It is typically harvested when the head reaches 16 to 20 centimeters in diameter.

nodes.name: siliques
nodes.summary: Siliques are a type of fruit that produce seeds containing round blackish seeds the size of No.9 shot, which can be harvested in August and remain good for eight or nine years.

nodes.name: France
nodes.summary: France is a country in which champignons naturally grow, particularly on lawns of dry meadows and woods, and at the foot of piles of horse manure.

nodes.name: petit salomon
nodes.summary: Petit salomon cauliflower forms apples around April 10 or 12; harvest when the head is as big as a hen's egg by breaking off lower leaves to deprive it of air and light.

nodes.name: gros salomon
nodes.summary: Gros salomon is a variety of cauliflower recognized by market gardeners as being larger and almost as early as petit salomon; apples form around April 10-12.

nodes.name: cotyledons
nodes.summary: Cotyledons are planted by hand up to the cotyledons in a basin made in the middle of each hole.

nodes.name: chests
nodes.summary: Chests are used in market gardening to cover the soil and for forced cultivation of sorrel.

nodes.name: planting board
nodes.summary: A tool used to press down firmly a 4-centimeter-thick bed of potting soil when transplanting cauliflower plants.

nodes.name: cauliflower plant
nodes.summary: Cauliflower plants are transplanted when they have 2 leaves, excluding cotyledons, and the soil is covered with a 4-centimeter-thick bed of potting soil.

nodes.name: wedges
nodes.summary: Wedges are used to hold open frames at the back when it is not freezing and remove them in the evening.

nodes.name: straw mats
nodes.summary: Straw mats are prepared and put in order by the end of the month to be used when needed; they are also used to cover the beds during frost and frames covered with straw mats when snow has fallen on them can be left as a shelter without melting.

nodes.name: covers of their bells and frames
nodes.summary: Covers of their bells and frames were used to protect forced crops from frost. Market gardeners would check the thermometer at night and double the covers if necessary.

nodes.name: air
nodes.summary: Air is necessary for market gardening in Paris; plants like chives, cabbage, and spinach require adequate air supply.

nodes.name: light
nodes.summary: Light is used in the cultivation of tomatoes, with seedlings being given light and air successively increased until May 1st, after which they can be planted indoors and outdoors, but only if there is no more frost.

nodes.name: Georges lettuce
nodes.summary: Georges lettuce is one of the first lettuces to be sold in August, along with black lettuces and gotte lettuces.

nodes.name: gotte lettuce
nodes.summary: Gotte lettuce is a type of lettuce sold in May by market gardeners, alongside other greens such as black lettuces and Georges lettuces.

nodes.name: straw pads
nodes.summary: used to raise boxes for cabbages and lettuces

nodes.name: D8 cabbage
nodes.summary: D8 cabbage requires increasing wetting as it grows and reaches maturity in April. The cauliflower needs to be covered by breaking off its lower leaves and applying them exactly onto the apple. This preserves whiteness by depriving air and light.

nodes.name: cauliflower head
nodes.summary: Cauliflower heads come in different varieties such as small Solomon, semi-hard cauliflower and hard cauliflower which grow at different rates and are affected by weather conditions.

nodes.name: semi-hard cauliflower
nodes.summary: Semi-hard cauliflower is planted in the open ground after 8 to 15 days of large salomon and 15 days before hard cauliflower.

nodes.name: hard cauliflower
nodes.summary: Hard cauliflower does very well in the open ground, and sprouts last, sometimes so late that frost catches it before it has formed.

nodes.name: forced-growth cabbages
nodes.summary: forced-growth cabbages are cultivated by sowing in the same time as others, and treated similarly throughout winter; they're stronger when planted due to longer exposure to air & light, and are typically planted in open ground at the end of Feb/beginning of March.

nodes.name: trays
nodes.summary: Trays are used by market gardeners to plant asparagus and force-growth cabbages, often filled with manure for warmth.

nodes.name: coast
nodes.summary: coast is a location for planting cabbages at the end of February and beginning of March to protect them from cold and keep them warm; also for romaine in marshes 8-15 days after coastal areas are planted.

nodes.name: nursery
nodes.summary: A place where plants are grown for transplanting; Alpine strawberry seedlings will flower and produce runners after being transplanted here.

nodes.name: larvae
nodes.summary: Larvae are a pest that can be present in the crown of cabbages, particularly those with protuberances.

nodes.name: coastal areas
nodes.summary: Coastal areas have different planting and care requirements for crops like cabbage due to exposure to winds and drying out; in November, begin to pick black lettuce planted on beds and under chasers.

nodes.name: beds in marshes
nodes.summary: Beds in marshes are planted with cabbages around 8-15 days after coastal areas have been planted.

nodes.name: square
nodes.summary: a location for planting and caring for cabbages, beds in marshes require a delayed start of 8-15 days after coastal areas have been planted due to differing shelter

nodes.name: thicker and shorter stem
nodes.summary: thicker and shorter stem is a characteristic of the semi-hard cauliflower variety.

nodes.name: bedding
nodes.summary: used for cauliflower cultivation; used as warm bedding to sow seeds of LARGE PUMPKIN and protecting cardoon from frost

nodes.name: AUTUMN CAULIFLOWER
nodes.summary: Autumn cauliflowers are ploughed and planted in the trenches after manure removal, and require abundant watering to produce beautiful results.

nodes.name: master gardener
nodes.summary: The master gardener draws nine or eleven lines or rows on the beds for planting cabbage, ensuring the soil is well tilled and mulched beforehand.

nodes.name: cabbage caterpillars
nodes.summary: Cabbage caterpillars multiply abundantly in certain years, causing considerable harm if not destroyed continually.

nodes.name: broccoli cabbage
nodes.summary: Broccoli cabbage contains several varieties, including white and purple broccoli. Paris market gardeners primarily cultivate these two varieties.

nodes.name: purple broccoli
nodes.summary: Purple broccoli is a variety of broccoli recognized by its darker leaf hue and violet apple color. It produces apples in summer and autumn if sown in April.

nodes.name: Broccoli
nodes.summary: Broccoli is brought to Paris from southern France and Finistère starting in February, creating competition for market gardeners.

nodes.name: BROCOLI BLANC
nodes.summary: BROCOLI BLANC is a type of cabbage grown in Paris. It is sown at the beginning of July and planted in the first days of August. It can withstand fairly heavy frosts and produces its head in the first spring.

nodes.name: leaf
nodes.summary: Leaf is a plant part; leaves of various plants like cardoon and others used in market gardening.

nodes.name: Solomon cauliflower (small)
nodes.summary: Small Solomon cauliflower variety; doesn't grow as large as large Solomon, produces head 8-15 days earlier.

nodes.name: Solomon cauliflower (large)
nodes.summary: Solomon cauliflower is sensitive to hot weather and doesn't grow well on the ground; it requires a bed and some soil.

nodes.name: Solomon cauliflower
nodes.summary: Solomon cauliflower is a type of cauliflower that produces its head eight to fifteen days earlier than large Solomon and hard cauliflower. It grows well in the open ground and its leaves are slightly more pointed than those of hard cauliflower.

nodes.name: open ground
nodes.summary: Open ground is used by market gardeners to grow pink radish seeds.

nodes.name: cellar
nodes.summary: A cellar is used to protect cardoons from frost and also for blanching unbound escarole and chicory by placing them root up.

nodes.name: tables
nodes.summary: Tables are used to lay aside cauliflower leaves for preservation.

nodes.name: nails
nodes.summary: Nails are used to hold cauliflowers in a cellar during winter.

nodes.name: floor joists
nodes.summary: Floor joists are used to hang cauliflower apples upside down after they've been hung in a specific way.

nodes.name: core
nodes.summary: Part of a plant used to tie cauliflower apples, helps preserve freshness when prepared properly.

nodes.name: table
nodes.summary: A piece of furniture for holding objects.

nodes.name: heavy rain
nodes.summary: Heavy rain is detrimental to cauliflower preservation; visit cauliflowers weekly to remove rotting leaves and check for staining.

nodes.name: fog
nodes.summary: Fog is a detrimental factor in preserving cauliflowers.

nodes.name: humidity
nodes.summary: Humidity is one of three causes of damping-off disease; too much humidity can lead to this disease in onions and sorrel.

nodes.name: embers
nodes.summary: Embers are used to dry the air in cellars by lighting a few of them when humidity appears.

nodes.name: fresh water
nodes.summary: used to immerse cauliflower cores for twenty-four or thirty-six hours without wetting the head

nodes.name: MACHE
nodes.summary: MACHE is a small, native, autumnal plant with two species: round and regency. It shows only leaves spread out in a rosette on the ground until spring, and then blooms its very small bluish flowers before dying.

nodes.name: valerian family
nodes.summary: The valerian family is a plant family that includes mache (lamb's lettuce), which has two species: round and regency.

nodes.name: fedia genus
nodes.summary: A plant of the valerian family and the fedia genus, includes round and regency species.

nodes.name: round mache
nodes.summary: Round mache is a small, native, autumnal plant of the valerian family and fedia genus. It has two species: round and regency. The round variety is also known as doucette.

nodes.name: Finistère
nodes.summary: Finistère is a region in south of France that sends broccoli to Paris, creating competition for the market gardeners.

nodes.name: doucette
nodes.summary: Doucette is a species of lamb's lettuce that grows easily from seed. Sow seeds in September on unploughed land or low ground, spreading compost over the seedbed and watering if dry.

nodes.name: seedbed
nodes.summary: A seedbed is a prepared area of soil used for growing seeds and young plants.

nodes.name: mâches
nodes.summary: Mâches are a type of lamb's lettuce that can be sown in August and picked from October to March; they are also picked towards the end of the month along with radishes and spinach.

nodes.name: October
nodes.summary: October is a month when planting of certain crops such as chives is more successful than in spring and we only repeat it every four or five years.

nodes.name: Calais
nodes.summary: Calais is a location mentioned in relation to market gardening operations.

nodes.name: Liliaceae
nodes.summary: Liliaceae is a plant family that includes plants such as leek or porreau and the genus Asparagus.

nodes.name: leeks
nodes.summary: Leeks are available in August and can be grown as autumn crops. They can be planted in trenches after the manure is removed.

nodes.name: fine compost
nodes.summary: 12-15 millimetres of fine compost used to sow lamb's lettuce; important to moisten soil for germination

nodes.name: porreaux
nodes.summary: Porreaux is a type of vegetable cultivated from December to May, grown using a layer of manure and terracotta.

nodes.name: teuf manure
nodes.summary: Teuf manure is used as fertilizer in market gardening; it is mixed with old manure to create a layer for sowing porreau seeds.

nodes.name: old manure
nodes.summary: used as fertilizer and amendment in market gardening; mixed with half new manure and wetted if necessary to develop the appropriate heat; layer must be 66 centimetres thick

nodes.name: terracotta
nodes.summary: Terracotta is a material used in market gardening for covering potting soil; it is added to the top of the layer, spread evenly throughout a trunk, to be about 8 centimeters thick.

nodes.name: plasterboard
nodes.summary: used to press down on seed and potting soil in a frame panel; applied over 1 cm of potting soil covering carrot seeds

nodes.name: porreau plant
nodes.summary: The porreau plant is ready for planting by mid-March. Its roots are cut to a length of 2 centimetres and the tips of its leaves are cut off so that the plant is no more than 18 centimetres long, then planted in soil covered with 15 millimetres of potting soil.

nodes.name: porreau
nodes.summary: Porreau is a plant that can be cultivated in spring and autumn. Spring porreau is ready for consumption at the end of May and beginning of June, while autumn porreau requires ploughing, harrowing, sowing seeds in March, then watering to help germination.

nodes.name: drought
nodes.summary: Drought can occur in Paris during dry springs and may require continued watering of plants such as chicory.

nodes.name: sunshine
nodes.summary: frost-free sunshine used to help germination and activate vegetation in autumn porreau cultivation

nodes.name: piglet
nodes.summary: A piglet is delivered for consumption in autumn and throughout the winter.

nodes.name: Umbelliferae family
nodes.summary: A plant family including species such as celery and celeriac.

nodes.name: genus
nodes.summary: The genus is a type of plant family that includes pea.

nodes.name: taproot
nodes.summary: Taproot is a characteristic of plants that makes them fairly drought-resistant. A plant part such as beet has a taproot.

nodes.name: compound leaves
nodes.summary: Compound leaves are used for pimpranelle.

nodes.name: plot
nodes.summary: plots a meter 33 centimeters wide; eleven or twelve rays along the length of the plots for sowing chervil seed

nodes.name: spring frosts
nodes.summary: Spring frosts do not damage chervil after it reaches a height of around 15 to 20 centimeters.

nodes.name: Compositae family
nodes.summary: The Compositae family is a group of plants that includes several species and varieties, including lettuce and tarragon, Scorzonera hispanica.

nodes.name: Petite Noire
nodes.summary: Petite Noire is a type of lettuce that has black seeds which retain germinative capacity for three or four years.

nodes.name: sideboard
nodes.summary: A shovel or sideboard is used to press down the potting soil.

nodes.name: light litter
nodes.summary: used to cover seeds with a straw mat or light litter in market gardening

nodes.name: manjère
nodes.summary: a method of transplanting seedlings

nodes.name: mould
nodes.summary: Mould is used in planting seedlings in the soil, after turning over and plumbing old bed or prepared beds for black lettuce.

nodes.name: root ball
nodes.summary: A small amount of root ball is left when lifting the seedling for planting; choosing a romaine plant with a nice root ball is important; when preserving cardoons in November, pull them out with part of their roots and a root ball if possible. When replanting celery, dig trenches 1 meter 33 centimeters wide and 35-38 centimeters deep; loosen the bottom by plowing; plant each celery with its root ball, making a dimple to receive it, and covering with earth slumped to set vertically.

nodes.name: mats
nodes.summary: Mats are used to protect plants from frost by covering them at night and on freezing days, and also as a dry litter or straw mats to cover trays of seedlings in greenhouses so that the heat does not evaporate.

nodes.name: laitue
nodes.summary: Laitue is planted under bells in a chessboard pattern, three rows at a time, and requires careful precautions.

nodes.name: feuilles de laitue
nodes.summary: Lettuce is a crop grown by market gardeners in Paris.

nodes.name: black lettuce
nodes.summary: Black lettuce is available in August and continues to be sold into May; it must be constantly replaced by new sowings so that the marsh remains full until autumn.

nodes.name: lettuce with black seed
nodes.summary: Lettuce with black seed is planted under each panel of the frame in November and December. It reaches full size at the end of these months and is tender but not as valuable as those grown in spring.

nodes.name: Petite Noire Lettuce
nodes.summary: Petite Noire Lettuce, also known as Small Black or Crepe Lettuce, is a type of lettuce that can be cultivated under frame and cloche without needing air.

nodes.name: letuce
nodes.summary: Lettuce is typically planted in boxes with 50 to 65 plants per panel at equal distances under each frame, and should be visited every eight days for inspection. Lettuce differs by color, size, and growth period - such as: Georges lettuce ready for harvesting at the end of March; Red lettuce with larger size and grown until June; Black lettuce that can peel under a bell without air.

nodes.name: insects
nodes.summary: Insects are a threat to various crops and can cause damage; examples include caterpillars that eat leaves and green aphids that besiege shoots.

nodes.name: molluscs
nodes.summary: Molluscs are pests that eat lettuce and can be removed every eight days.

nodes.name: Black or crepe lettuce
nodes.summary: Black or crepe lettuce is sown from October 20 to 25, and receives the same care and treatment as small black lettuce in forced cultivation.

nodes.name: gros salomon cauliflower
nodes.summary: Gros salomon cauliflower is a type of cauliflower planted along with lettuces on the same beds.

nodes.name: lettuce plant
nodes.summary: Lettuce plants are protected from frost by covering them at night and using straw mats, then removing the covers during sunny periods to allow the sun's rays to penetrate.

nodes.name: afternoon
nodes.summary: afternoon is mentioned in relation to taking care of lettuce plants during frosty weather.

nodes.name: bonnet of the bells
nodes.summary: A covering used to protect lettuce plants from frost.

nodes.name: lettuce beds
nodes.summary: Lettuce beds are made by covering the bells at night with straw mats and placing short, dry manure between them to a thickness of 10-12mm, later adding compost; they consist of 3 rows of bells with 4 black lettuces under each bell and one romaine in the center, atop 32cm thick manure filled with 10cm compost.

nodes.name: romaine lettuce
nodes.summary: Romaine lettuce overwinters without shelter and is given a hoeing around Holy Week; in August we sow the three types of romaine, but the green one will end with the month. Romaine can be sown in June after cauliflowers.

nodes.name: atmospheric temperature
nodes.summary: Depending on atmospheric temperature, melons may need to be watered every five or six days if the weather is fine and sunny.

nodes.name: three-pronged fork
nodes.summary: A three-pronged fork is used to harrow the soil in order to hide rapunzel seeds and compact the earth so that it touches the seed on all sides.

nodes.name: cloche
nodes.summary: A cloche is used to cover layers on which lettuces and romaines are planted under frame in March; also used for melons.

nodes.name: crêpe lettuce
nodes.summary: Crêpe lettuce is harvested after petite noire lettuces and its soil is ploughed with a fork in February.

nodes.name: Red Lettuce
nodes.summary: Red Lettuce differs from Georges lettuce in its red color and larger size, grown until June. It's sown around October 15th on teas, then transplanted under cloche.

nodes.name: atmosphere
nodes.summary: The state of the atmosphere is considered in market gardening, for example a sudden cooling of the atmosphere caused by thunder can kill fungus grain.

nodes.name: GREY LETTUCE
nodes.summary: Grey lettuce is a variety of lettuce that can be grown in September and throughout the summer.

nodes.name: conch
nodes.summary: Conch is a surface used for sowing lettuce seeds and does not require transplantation.

nodes.name: cutting lettuce
nodes.summary: Cutting lettuce is a type of lettuce that does not head and has a white, long seed. It's sown in February and March on a warm bed and cut when 4 to 5 centimetres high for use in salads.

nodes.name: Passion lettuce
nodes.summary: Passion lettuce is not cultivated by Paris market gardeners and is only grown in kitchen gardens. It is a hardy plant that can overwinter without shelter.

nodes.name: kitchen gardens
nodes.summary: Kitchen gardens are mentioned as places where lettuces such as Passion lettuce and Romaine lettuce are grown without forced cultivation.

nodes.name: Chicon
nodes.summary: Chicon is a type of lettuce also known as Romaine

nodes.name: Holy Week
nodes.summary: Holy Week is a period in spring when Passion lettuce is given a hoeing and apples.

nodes.name: lettuce genus
nodes.summary: Lettuce genus; forms a column 20 to 25 centimeters high, with long, narrow leaves of a stronger consistency.

nodes.name: round leaves
nodes.summary: Round leaves are a part of lettuce plants, pressed together and forming a rounded head.

nodes.name: ROGIAINE VERTE DITE DE PRINCEUR
nodes.summary: ROGIAINE VERTE DITE DE PRINCEUR is a type of romaine lettuce suitable for forced cultivation, grown by Paris market gardeners until the beginning of May.

nodes.name: little black lettuce
nodes.summary: Little black lettuce is a type of lettuce that can be sown in September to be planted under a bell or cold frame.

nodes.name: green romaine
nodes.summary: Green romaine is a type of lettuce that can be harvested in July and replaces escarole; it continues to be sown along with other vegetables like turnips, radishes, and chervil.

nodes.name: old bed
nodes.summary: old bed refers to a piece of land that has been used for previous crops and is also a term used in market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: romaine seedlings
nodes.summary: Romaine seedlings are transplanted under cloches after being lifted; 24-30 seedlings per cloche.

nodes.name: romaine plant
nodes.summary: Romaine plant is susceptible to black aphid, white aphid, red-headed chafer, miller disease, red collet, and melting; requires care during frost threats by making supports around beds, replacing cauliflower transplanted onto a frame.

nodes.name: green romaine plants
nodes.summary: Green romaine plants are selected in January and February for planting under cloches on lettuce beds to produce forced romaine for consumption in the first week of March.

nodes.name: porridge seed
nodes.summary: Porridge seed is sown in the coast and then covered with compost, usually 1 cm thick.

nodes.name: Grey romain
nodes.summary: Grey romain is a type of lettuce that is larger and more sensitive to frost than green romaine; it is sown at the end of October, transplanted in the same way on ados and under cloche, protected from frost, and planted in full squares in March.

nodes.name: parsley seed
nodes.summary: Parsley seed germinates in spring and ripens its fruit in June; oval, longitudinally striated, 2 millimeters long, didymous or dividing into two bean-shaped parts, each containing a seed. Harvesting parsley berries is straightforward: they retain their germinative capacity for four or five years.

nodes.name: grey romaine
nodes.summary: Grey romaine is a type of lettuce that can be resown in November, transplanted onto ados as previously mentioned, and is prone to speckling in the center if watered during very hot weather.

nodes.name: heart
nodes.summary: The heart of beef is a type of cabbage mentioned in Chapter X as being available in September; it can be tied and removed to hide the heart, then trimmed and sent to the market when ready.

nodes.name: BLOND ROMAINE
nodes.summary: Blond romaine lettuce is available in September.

nodes.name: Gray romaine
nodes.summary: Gray romaine is harvested in September along with blond and red lettuces, sweetbread and beef cabbage.

nodes.name: Blonde Romaine
nodes.summary: Blonde romaine is a type of lettuce that can be sown in October and November, transplanted under cold frames, and planted in squares from March to July. It requires similar care as gray romaine.

nodes.name: speckling
nodes.summary: speckling is a problem that occurs in blond romaine lettuce in very hot weather if watered during the middle of the day.

nodes.name: blonde lettuces
nodes.summary: Blonde lettuces are prone to speckling in the center if watered during hot weather.

nodes.name: Gotte
nodes.summary: Gotte is a type of lettuce in Paris market gardening. It can be sown under cloche in August to be transplanted onto teens and under cloche or under chassis.

nodes.name: Solomon's cauliflower
nodes.summary: Solomon's cauliflower is a type of cauliflower that is small and half-hard to hard. It is typically transplanted under frames in November.

nodes.name: collar
nodes.summary: The collar of cardoon is trimmed after it has been cut by the root and de-swaddled.

nodes.name: pain-de-sucre cabbages
nodes.summary: pain-de-sucre cabbages are planted in autumn

nodes.name: legume family
nodes.summary: includes plants of the legume family and genus, such as haricot beans with ternate leaves and spiral flowers

nodes.name: MICHAUX PEAS FROM HOLLAND
nodes.summary: MICHAUX PEAS FROM HOLLAND is a pea variety chosen for cultivation under frame due to its earliness and suitability.

nodes.name: Prince Albert pea
nodes.summary: Prince Albert pea is a variety of pea that competes for earliness with the Michaux pea from Holland.

nodes.name: melon boxes
nodes.summary: used to grow early peas around March 15th; prepared by ploughing beds in November, dividing the soil, surrounding with melon boxes, sowing five rows of peas, filling paths with cold old manure, and using straw mats;

nodes.name: straw plugs
nodes.summary: used to elevate boxes by placing them under the corners to maintain a space of about 33 centimetres from the top of the asparagus plants

nodes.name: hâtive
nodes.summary: hâtive is a type of little black lettuce that grows well under cloche and chassis, requiring moderate heat.

nodes.name: horseradish genus
nodes.summary: Horseradish genus is a cruciferous family plant that includes varieties like rave and radish, characterized by annual plants, spreading leaves, and edible roots in various colors such as white, pink, violet or black.

nodes.name: Rave
nodes.summary: Rave is an annual plant of the cruciferous family, cultivated for its edible taproot. Varieties include early violet rabe and pink demi-long radish.

nodes.name: Auricles
nodes.summary: Auricles are also known as coquilletons; in market gardening, not all gardeners agree on whether to remove them or only if they see rot; some leave them.

nodes.name: Hative Violet Rabe
nodes.summary: Hative Violet Rabe is a type of rabe plant used for forced cultivations. It is more advantageous and sought-after, making it the preferred choice.

nodes.name: Pink demi-long radish
nodes.summary: Pink demi-long radish is a variety of radish. It is an annual plant with spreading, pinnatifid leaves at the base and stems growing to a height of about a meter. The root is simple, taprooted, fleshy, fusiform or rounded, white, pink, violet or black. The roots alone are edible and eaten raw.

nodes.name: rabe
nodes.summary: Rabe is a crop grown in frames and needs air; it should be covered to prevent frost from penetrating.

nodes.name: stake
nodes.summary: A good 1-metre-high stake is screwed to each tomato plant and the prepared stems are tied to it with a strand of straw.

nodes.name: frost-free haze
nodes.summary: A weather condition characterized by a lack of frost.

nodes.name: crucifer seedlings
nodes.summary: Crucifer seedlings are susceptible to aphids (flea beetles) that devour them in dry springs.

nodes.name: BLACK RADISH
nodes.summary: Black radish seeds are collected when eaten, keep well for four years, sown in June, and harvested like pink radishes.

nodes.name: small white prescott
nodes.summary: Small white prescott is a type of melon that can be sown as early as December 20th or 25th and produces ripe fruit around April 15th. It prefers thermosiphon heating in forced cultivations under chassis. Grown by Mr. Gontier in Petit-Montrouge, Paris.

nodes.name: thermosiphon
nodes.summary: A heating system used by market gardeners in Paris for forced cultivations under chassis; its advantages outweigh the expense of installation and maintenance.

nodes.name: frosty
nodes.summary: frosty weather may require special composting arrangements

nodes.name: dead or defective leaves
nodes.summary: dead or defective leaves need to be removed from plants in cold weather.

nodes.name: Umbelliferae
nodes.summary: A plant family including parsley and carrots

nodes.name: fruits
nodes.summary: Fruits are part of plants' growth and development in Paris market gardening, specifically for black radish and raiponce.

nodes.name: winter beds
nodes.summary: Winter beds are used for planting early melons in February by breaking up the soil covering old winter layers and mixing it with new manure.

nodes.name: Mr. Gontier
nodes.summary: Mr. Gontier is a market gardener who uses the thermosiphon device on his fermented crops.

nodes.name: fumier de cheval
nodes.summary: fumier de cheval is mentioned as a substance for growing champignons

nodes.name: février
nodes.summary: February is mentioned as the time when market gardeners in Paris typically sow their first melons.

nodes.name: cucurbitaceae family
nodes.summary: a plant family that includes melons and cucumbers, characterized by annual plants with slender roots

nodes.name: cucumber genus
nodes.summary: Cucumber is prone to melting, especially if sown thickly; same enemies as melons.

nodes.name: slender roots
nodes.summary: Slender roots of melons trace, belonging to the cucurbitaceae family and cucumber genus.

nodes.name: ovary
nodes.summary: The ovary is a part of a female flower in the cucurbitaceae family, recognizable by its large olive-shaped appearance below the flower.

nodes.name: olive-shaped ovary
nodes.summary: a large olive-shaped ovary below the female flower of a melon plant

nodes.name: false flower
nodes.summary: In gardening, the male flower of melons and cucumbers is called a false flower.

nodes.name: male flower of melons and cucumbers
nodes.summary: The male flower of melons and cucumbers is known as a false flower in gardening; it lacks an ovary and appears before the female flowers.

nodes.name: maille
nodes.summary: Maille is a type of female flower in melons and cucumbers.

nodes.name: brodé melon
nodes.summary: Brodé melon is a variety of melon mentioned as being grown by market gardeners in Paris alongside maraîcher melon and cantaloupe; it is an ancient species inferior to cantaloupes in price and quality and has different cultivation methods.

nodes.name: maraîcher melon
nodes.summary: Maraîcher melon is a type of melon grown in Paris and has been largely replaced by cantaloupes. Its cultivation was once simple but now involves new processes to produce cantaloupes.

nodes.name: orange melon
nodes.summary: A type of melon that is prized for its earliness but often too small to be profitable; seeds are oblong, compressed, leathery-skinned, yellowish-white and smooth, 11mm long and 6mm wide.

nodes.name: market garden melon
nodes.summary: Market garden melon is a type of vegetable that has many varieties, including cantaloupe. Pruning is done above the second leaf and it takes 40-50 days to ripen from being tied.

nodes.name: Paris market
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris, with various varieties of vegetables and plants grown including cantaloupe, melon, spinach, cabbage, and onions.

nodes.name: nursery layer
nodes.summary: A layer of potting soil used for transplanting seedlings, set up with a frame and covered with a mat to maintain temperature, typically filled with 10 centimetres of potting soil.

nodes.name: mother layer
nodes.summary: a layer of soil for seedlings in forced cultivation, often used to start cucumbers in February

nodes.name: nursery bed
nodes.summary: A nursery bed is used for transplanting seedlings; has holes filled with manure and covered with soil, and is used to lift pumpkin plants with a good root ball for planting.

nodes.name: pot
nodes.summary: used for transplanting plants

nodes.name: Prescott Melon
nodes.summary: Prescott Melon is a type of melon grown for early production. It is preferred to be transplanted in potting soil rather than direct planting.

nodes.name: wheelbarrow
nodes.summary: A wheelbarrow is used to transport pots of melons from the nursery bed to the planting area.

nodes.name: dry manure
nodes.summary: used as fertilizer and to fill paths

nodes.name: Petit prescott melons
nodes.summary: Petit prescott melons are planted in February and can produce ripe fruit from May 10 to 15 if the year is not too unfavorable.

nodes.name: cantaloupe gros prescott fond noir
nodes.summary: Cantaloupe variety preferred by Paris market gardeners for second-season cultivation in trenches; sown from February 20 to 25 and transplanted to nursery bed.

nodes.name: cantaloupe gros prescott fond blanc
nodes.summary: Cantaloupe variety preferred by Paris market gardeners; sown from February 20 to 25 and transplanted to nursery bed for second-season cultivation; melon maraicher is being replaced in favor of cantaloupes.

nodes.name: prescott cantaloupe
nodes.summary: A type of cantaloupe mentioned in the context of market gardening in Paris. It can be planted on trenched beds and comes in different varieties such as small prescott, white-bottomed large prescott, and black- or gray-bottomed.

nodes.name: trenched beds
nodes.summary: Trenched beds are used for planting small prescott cantaloupe and other varieties of cantaloupe. The manure placed in the paths is called rechaud and around the layers is called accot.

nodes.name: mother bed
nodes.summary: A mother bed is sometimes used for tomatoes by sowing tomato seed in a warmed up widowed or reworked bed at the end of March.

nodes.name: cantaloup gros galeux vert
nodes.summary: Cantaloup gros galeux vert is a type of melon that can be grown in Paris and has been written about by market gardeners J. G. Moreau and Jardinier-Maraicher J. Davenne.

nodes.name: flexible litter
nodes.summary: Flexible litter is used by market gardeners to shade melon plants, placed on top of each bell.

nodes.name: embroidered melon
nodes.summary: The embroidered melon is a type of melon that is sown at the beginning of May, transplanted around the 10th or 12th, and planted in place towards the end of the month. It is often grown on beds made like those of fourth-season cantaloupes.

nodes.name: fever
nodes.summary: Fever is associated with eating melons in a certain season.

nodes.name: pruning
nodes.summary: Pruning is done two times: after the fourth leaf; and finally to spread out branches and remove yellow leaves.

nodes.name: coquilletons
nodes.summary: coquilletons refer to the auricles of a plant; not all gardeners agree on removing them; some leave them, while others remove them if they see rot; J.G Moreau and J.Davern always remove the two cotyledons and any buds they may have in their axils at the same time as topping them on the nursery bed.

nodes.name: sunny day
nodes.summary: a sunny day is ideal for pruning melons in market gardening

nodes.name: branches
nodes.summary: Branches of plants, such as those used for harvesting lettuce seeds.

nodes.name: _branch
nodes.summary: Branches are pruned in stages, with the fourth pruning done above the second leaf and the fifth pruning cutting off branches born after the fourth pruning above their first leaf.

nodes.name: growth
nodes.summary: Growth of market garden produce in Paris is described, noting the need for practical experience and tact to recognize when a mesh will produce a beautiful fruit.

nodes.name: green
nodes.summary: Green is mentioned as a color related to melon growth ("the fresh tone of its green are good omens").

nodes.name: root tips
nodes.summary: Root tips of melons need moisture after fruit has set

nodes.name: leaves of the melons
nodes.summary: leaves of melons need moisture after fruit has set.

nodes.name: melon maraîcher
nodes.summary: Melon maraîcher is a type of melon cultivated by Paris market gardeners, typically sown and transplanted at the end of April or early May, on spring bed with two rows planted 33 centimeters from the edge.

nodes.name: Cucurbitaceae
nodes.summary: Cucurbitaceae is a plant family that includes the squash genus; pumpkin varieties are annual, creeping plants with large leaves and tendrils.

nodes.name: WHITE HATIF CUCUMBER
nodes.summary: White Hatif Cucumber is a type of cucumber that is grown for its unique shape and flavor. It differs from the melon in that its fruits are only eaten cooked or candied and there are fewer varieties of it.

nodes.name: motté
nodes.summary: a good motté is used to plant early cucumbers at a suitable distance from the wood and between them

nodes.name: vine
nodes.summary: a plant part that needs to be pruned to encourage a cucumber plant's growth

nodes.name: cucumber plant
nodes.summary: Cucumber plants are grown in light, warm, fertile and sheltered soil in Parisian "côtières". Seedlings are raised on beds and transplanted around May 15th.

nodes.name: côtières
nodes.summary: A type of cultivation done by Parisian market gardeners, typically undertaken in light, warm, fertile and sheltered soil.

nodes.name: Compositae
nodes.summary: Compositae is a family of plants that includes annuals and perennials, some growing up to 1 meter tall, including the cardoon plant.

nodes.name: shoots
nodes.summary: Shoots grow, branch out and flower; wetting them is essential during flowering to prevent aphid infestation.

nodes.name: chicory plant
nodes.summary: Chicory plant is transplanted 12 days after sowing, with 350-400 plants per panel, and requires protection from cold.

nodes.name: wood of the trunk
nodes.summary: 16 centimetres from the wood of the trunk

nodes.name: rootlets
nodes.summary: Rootlets are part of a plant's root system and are important for its growth. When transplanting chicory, it is essential to handle the seedling carefully and ensure that plenty of compost remains between its rootlets.

nodes.name: autumn chicory
nodes.summary: Autumn chicory has finely cut leaves and is harvested at around 22 centimeters wide when suitably white.

nodes.name: mulch boards
nodes.summary: Mulch boards are used in cultivating chicory.

nodes.name: blanks
nodes.summary: No specific information about blanks was found in the text.

nodes.name: Meaux chicory
nodes.summary: Meaux chicory is planted in autumn after the melon trenches are emptied and ploughed.

nodes.name: herbalists
nodes.summary: Herbalists buy chicory leaves from market gardeners during the summer.

nodes.name: Capital's markets
nodes.summary: Markets in Paris receive chicory from growers from the end of October to the end of January.

nodes.name: Curled-leaf or Round-leaf Endive
nodes.summary: Curled-leaf or Round-leaf Endive is classified as an escarole in gardening and has characteristics of chicories. It's fatter and more plump than some varieties; its salad is less tough than chicory.

nodes.name: Scarole
nodes.summary: Scarole is sown for seed production in August and September, marking the most beautiful plants and protecting them from frost during winter.

nodes.name: HARICOTS
nodes.summary: HARICOTS are frequently subject to gray mold in forced and natural cultivation, and loaches and slugs eat its leaves in wet weather.

nodes.name: ternate leaves
nodes.summary: Ternate leaves are a type of leaf structure composed of three leaflets and are characteristic of plants in the legume family, such as haricot beans.

nodes.name: leaflets
nodes.summary: Leaflets are a part of plants' leaves with broad, lobed or incised shapes in the context of parsnip cultivation.

nodes.name: carina
nodes.summary: Carina is a plant part of legume family plants and has spiral-contoured stamens and style.

nodes.name: style
nodes.summary: style refers to a variety of bean suitable for forcing, particularly the Flageolet bean or Dutch dwarf.

nodes.name: Dutch dwarf
nodes.summary: The Dutch dwarf is also known as Laon dwarf and the earliest suitable for forcing in Paris market gardening.

nodes.name: Laon dwarf
nodes.summary: Laon dwarf is an early and suitable bean for forcing

nodes.name: Dutch dwarf bean
nodes.summary: The Dutch dwarf bean is an early and suitable variety for forcing. It is sown in February, takes 8-10 days to sprout, and grows quickly.

nodes.name: wooden wedges
nodes.summary: Wooden wedges are used to control airflow for plants under frames.

nodes.name: fruits (small green beans)
nodes.summary: Fruits (small green beans) need to be picked every three days when they are 6-7 centimeters long.

nodes.name: RARICOT FLAGEOLET
nodes.summary: A type of bean that is cultivated in open ground under a frame from the last fortnight of April to July.

nodes.name: PERSIL
nodes.summary: PERSIL is a biennial, tap-rooted plant of the Umbelliferae family and genus, about 1 meter high, with decomposed radical leaves: whitish, umbellate flowers, followed by geminate, oval, ridged fruits. Market gardeners of Paris grow only the bloud and vert varieties.

nodes.name: tap-rooted plant
nodes.summary: a biennial plant, about 1 meter high, radical leaves used as a condiment in cooking

nodes.name: radical leaves
nodes.summary: Radical leaves are edible parts of the raiponce plant, eaten in salads.

nodes.name: whitish, umbellate flowers
nodes.summary: whitish, umbellate flowers are a part of the parsley plant and are used in cooking

nodes.name: geminate, oval, ridged fruits
nodes.summary: Geminate, oval, ridged fruits are part of the parsley plant.

nodes.name: bloud parsley
nodes.summary: Bloud parsley is a biennial, tap-rooted plant of the Umbelliferae family and of the genus whose name it bears; it is about 1 meter high, with decomposed radical leaves: whitish, umbellate flowers, followed by geminate, oval, ridged fruits. Market gardeners of Paris value the vert parsley more than the blond.

nodes.name: vert parsley
nodes.summary: Vert parsley is a variety of parsley valued more than the blond by market gardeners in Paris.

nodes.name: ASPERGE
nodes.summary: Asparagus is a perennial plant of the Liliaceae family, used in recipes like Chives, civets, appetizers

nodes.name: rhizome
nodes.summary: a large perennial rhizome, commonly called a claw or paw, from which grow annual, stalky stems

nodes.name: claw or paw
nodes.summary: a large perennial rhizome

nodes.name: Hollande asparagus
nodes.summary: Hollande asparagus is a species of asparagus mentioned in the context of market gardening in Paris. It is recommended to be sown in March in a bed of good, loose soil and requires weeding and watering as needed. The plant can be ready for planting after one year.

nodes.name: Marchiennes asparagus
nodes.summary: Marchiennes asparagus is a species of asparagus, cultivated by broadcasting its seed in good, loose soil in March and covering it with potting soil.

nodes.name: bed of good, loose soil
nodes.summary: a bed of good, loose soil, neither in the shade nor too hot, for sowing asparagus seeds

nodes.name: hot spot
nodes.summary: A hot spot is an area that is too warm for a specific activity or cultivation.

nodes.name: seed of asparagus
nodes.summary: Seed of asparagus, specifically Hollande or Marchiennes, is planted in loose soil in March, watered and weeded, then transplanted after a year.

nodes.name: best soil from the pit
nodes.summary: 8 centimeters of best soil from pit is used to compact well after putting cow manure

nodes.name: pit for planting
nodes.summary: 1.35m wide and 10cm deep pit prepared for planting asparagus, filled with 20cm of well-pressed cow manure and covered with 8cm of compacted soil.

nodes.name: pit
nodes.summary: A small oblong pit is used for marcotting; it is 16 centimeters deep and used to develop roots on the stem of a plant.

nodes.name: claw
nodes.summary: A claw is a tool used for planting asparagus, produces 10 green asparagus plants on average, with panels containing 450-500 claws producing 4,500 to 5,000 green asparagus plants.

nodes.name: pitchfork
nodes.summary: A pitchfork is used for small ploughing and arranging manure in a trench when making spawn.

nodes.name: asparagus patch
nodes.summary: Asparagus is attacked by criocer, a red insect that eats the bark of stalks, and white grubs and moths torment claws in the ground.

nodes.name: asparagus boards
nodes.summary: used to force white asparagus; fill paths of the asparagus patch with well-divided soil and new horse manure

nodes.name: claw or rhizome
nodes.summary: Part of an asparagus plant from which new growth emerges. Harvested white asparagus should be about 24 centimetres long.

nodes.name: open air
nodes.summary: Asparagus grown in the open air in spring.

nodes.name: asparagus plant
nodes.summary: White asparagus can be harvested every two days for two months; plant must not be picked again in spring if forced over winter.

nodes.name: Les Vertus
nodes.summary: Les Vertus is a location in France where growers raise asparagus.

nodes.name: Saint-Denis
nodes.summary: Saint-Denis is mentioned as one of the locations outside the capital of Paris where growers raise green asparagus in open fields.

nodes.name: Saint-Quen
nodes.summary: Saint-Quen is a location on the outskirts of Paris where growers raise asparagus in open fields.

nodes.name: urine
nodes.summary: Used as a fertilizer mixed with manure for forcing green asparagus in October

nodes.name: hot atmosphere
nodes.summary: A hot atmosphere is created under the frames when warm humidity rises from the manure, penetrating the asparagus claws and stimulating their growth.

nodes.name: pea asparagus
nodes.summary: Sold in 52-centimeter bundles; cultivated by a small number of Paris market gardeners. Asparagus is prone to insects like criocer and white grubs, and diseases.

nodes.name: selanum genus
nodes.summary: The selanum genus is a part of the solanaceae family and is native to the Chands.

nodes.name: Chands
nodes.summary: The Chands is mentioned as the origin of eggplant plants.

nodes.name: twig
nodes.summary: Twig is a part of a plant.

nodes.name: fogils
nodes.summary: Fogils are a type of plant part from the Solanaceae family and Selanum genus, with large, oval, tomentose leaves.

nodes.name: calyx
nodes.summary: The calyx of lettuces holds seeds, which are harvested as they ripen and detach from the stem.

nodes.name: eggplant seed
nodes.summary: Eggplant seed is sown in a mother bed with compost and covered with potting soil. It sprouts in four days and is transplanted to a nursery bed 8-10 days later. Eggplants grow slowly.

nodes.name: April 15
nodes.summary: April 15 is mentioned as a date for planting eggplants in the ground, in a coastal or sheltered spot, and covering each plant with a cloche.

nodes.name: coastal spot
nodes.summary: coastal spot in Paris used for growing eggplants with a cloche until the end of May

nodes.name: sheltered spot
nodes.summary: A sheltered spot is used for planting eggplants in late April (around 15th to 20th), covering each plant with a cloche and lifting it at the right moment with a cré-maillère to give it air, typically until the end of May.

nodes.name: cré-maillère
nodes.summary: A tool used to provide air for plants grown under cloches.

nodes.name: Tomato or Pomme d'amour
nodes.summary: A plant of the solanaceae family and solanum genus, native to South America

nodes.name: solanaceae family
nodes.summary: a plant family that includes solanum genus and is native to South America

nodes.name: solanum genus
nodes.summary: A plant of the solanaceae family and solanum genus, native to South America. Annual, twiggy, diffuse, grows over 1 meter high by means of support.

nodes.name: South America
nodes.summary: South America is mentioned as the native place of the solanum genus and tomatoes.

nodes.name: Solanaceae
nodes.summary: Solanaceae is a plant family native to South America and includes species such as tomato (Solanum genus).

nodes.name: Pomme d'Amour
nodes.summary: Pomme d'Amour, also known as Tomato, is a plant of the solanaceae family and solanum genus, native to South America.

nodes.name: Twisted fruit
nodes.summary: The twisted fruit is a type of tomato that grows to be red, rounded and succulent. It is an edible part of the plant.

nodes.name: Winged, incised leaves
nodes.summary: Leaves of the tomato plant are winged and incised.

nodes.name: Dirty-white flowers
nodes.summary: Small, dirty-white flowers arranged in simple clusters.

nodes.name: tomato seeds
nodes.summary: Tomato seeds are sown by market gardeners in early March on old beds, 1 foot apart, or at the end of March in full squares or on sides, with plants placed 66 cm apart.

nodes.name: Trellis
nodes.summary: used for supporting tomato plants

nodes.name: PERCE-PIERRE
nodes.summary: Perce-Pierre is a perennial plant of the Umbelliferae family, native to Europe, with herbaceous and twiggy stems, used in salad supplies and pickled in vinegar.

nodes.name: Seselina
nodes.summary: Seselina is a perennial plant of the Umbelliferae family with a herbaceous stem and small whitish flowers, used in salad supplies and pickled in vinegar.

nodes.name: Salad supplies
nodes.summary: Salad supplies include perce-pierre (a native perennial plant), pepper (originated in India and introduced into Europe around two hundred years ago).

nodes.name: Pickles preserved in vinegar
nodes.summary: Pickles preserved in vinegar are used along with leaves and young stems of Perce-Pierre.

nodes.name: Sea Stone Borer
nodes.summary: A plant pest that can be sown in March at the foot of a wall facing east or west, penetrating the wall and sprouting stems, leaves, and flowers every year; also cultivated in borders like tarragon.

nodes.name: Pepper
nodes.summary: Peppers are mentioned in the context of being harvested along with other vegetables such as chicory, escarole, cauliflower, and more.

nodes.name: Solana family
nodes.summary: A plant family that originated in India and was introduced into Europe around two hundred years ago, known for the pepper genus.

nodes.name: India
nodes.summary: India is a country in Asia where the chili pepper originated two hundred years ago.

nodes.name: Europe
nodes.summary: Europe is a continent that is home to several species of strawberry plants and is one of the locations mentioned in the context of market gardening.

nodes.name: Coral Pepper
nodes.summary: Coral Pepper is a plant of the solana family that originated in India and was introduced into Europe around two hundred years ago.

nodes.name: Arroche
nodes.summary: Arroche is an annual plant from Asia in the Chenopoda family. It has triangular leaves that are edible.

nodes.name: Asie
nodes.summary: Asie is a location mentioned in the text as the origin of an annual plant.

nodes.name: feuilles
nodes.summary: Feuilles are leaves used in market gardening for plants such as Bonne-Dame.

nodes.name: GARDEN LADY'S SLIPPER
nodes.summary: Garden Lady's Slipper is an annual plant of the Chenopoda family and Arroche genus. It grows up to a meter high, has insignificant flowers, and produces small edible leaves.

nodes.name: marché
nodes.summary: Market gardening in Paris

nodes.name: red stem and leaves variety of BONNE-DAME
nodes.summary: Red stem and leaves variety of BONNE-DAME that is just as good and requires no more care.

nodes.name: meters
nodes.summary: unit of measurement

nodes.name: PIMPRENELLE
nodes.summary: PIMPRENELLE is a perennial plant in the Rosaceae family. Its edible leaves are used to garnish salads and cultivated by Paris market gardeners in small quantities. The plant has compound radical leaves, and its stem reaches about 5 decimeters high.

nodes.name: Rosaceae family
nodes.summary: The Rosaceae family includes a plant, strawberry, of the Dryadae order and of the genus whose name it bears.

nodes.name: Sanguisorbia
nodes.summary: Sanguisorbia is a tribe in the Rosaceae family that includes plants such as pimpernelle, which has edible leaves used to garnish salads.

nodes.name: Poterium
nodes.summary: Poterium is a small perennial plant of the Rosaceae family used by Paris market gardeners for its edible leaves in salads.

nodes.name: monoecious flowers
nodes.summary: Monoecious flowers of Pimprenelle plant are ball-shaped, small, and have tetragonal seeds.

nodes.name: tetragonal seeds
nodes.summary: Small tetragonal seeds from Pimpernelle plant in Paris, cultivated by market gardeners for garnishing salads.

nodes.name: ESTRAGON
nodes.summary: Estragon's only known disease is a kind of canker that appears as a yellowish spot at the foot of the plant.

nodes.name: Artemisia section
nodes.summary: Artemisia section is a perennial plant native to Tartary, used for garnishing salads.

nodes.name: Artemisia genus
nodes.summary: Artemisia genus is a perennial plant native to Tartary, used for its edible young stems and tender leaves in salads.

nodes.name: Tartary
nodes.summary: Tartary is a region native to the perennial plant Artemisia.

nodes.name: ALENOIS CRESSON
nodes.summary: ALENOIS CRESSON is an annual plant of the Cruciferae family with small white flowers and edible lower leaves used in salads.

nodes.name: Cruciferae family
nodes.summary: The Cruciferae family is mentioned as a section that includes the genus Thlaspi and an annual plant of unknown origin with small, sharply-cut leaves.

nodes.name: Lepidinae section
nodes.summary: Lepidinae is a section of the Cruciferae family, comprising plants such as alenois cress.

nodes.name: cress alénois
nodes.summary: Cress alénois is cultivated in marshes.

nodes.name: double cress
nodes.summary: Double cress is cultivated in small quantities in marshes in Paris; it has curled and non-curled leaf varieties. Seeds are sown in late April or early May in rows, then covered with potting soil. The plant grows quickly, typically harvested when leaves reach 8-10 centimeters high, with possible second harvest before going to seed.

nodes.name: polygon family
nodes.summary: a plant family that includes sorrel and has perennial species

nodes.name: geurea
nodes.summary: Geurea is a genus of plants in the polygon family, some species are perennial and more or less acidic.

nodes.name: petioles
nodes.summary: Petioles are a part of celery plants and can be harvested; they require shade for planting, regular watering to prevent hardening, and transplanting when 10-14cm high

nodes.name: heart leaves
nodes.summary: small heart leaves can be harvested in their turn a fortnight after main harvest.

nodes.name: Virgin Sorrel
nodes.summary: Virgin Sorrel is a type of plant with broader, rounder leaves that are blonder green and less acidic than common sorrel. It is typically planted in beds 16 centimeters apart and replanted every four or five years. Virgin Sorrel can be cultivated through forced cultivation by placing it in chests with at least 16 centimeters of compost. The best time to plant is in October, as planting in spring is less successful.

nodes.name: Clover
nodes.summary: Clover is a plant that can be cultivated in beds.

nodes.name: POTIRON
nodes.summary: POTIRON is a plant of the Cucurbitaceae family and squash genus. It has several species or varieties, all annual, creeping plants with large leaves and tendrils.

nodes.name: LARGE PUMPKIN
nodes.summary: LARGE PUMPKIN is grown by sowing its seed on warm bedding in early April and transplanting it to a nursery after 8 days. It requires protection from frost at night. By the first week of May, the plant is ready for planting, requiring holes 1 meter square and 55 cm deep filled with well-packed manure and covered with soil. A similar cultivation process can be used for smaller varieties such as numbers 3, 4, and 5.

nodes.name: main stem
nodes.summary: The main stem is allowed to grow until it reaches a certain length, then marcotting is performed by removing lower leaves and covering the node in soil to stimulate root growth.

nodes.name: sap
nodes.summary: The sap is directed into the main stem by removing offshoots.

nodes.name: flesh
nodes.summary: pale flesh; also red-fleshed, green, Turkish bonnet or turban pumpkin, and small Jerusalem artichoke pumpkin

nodes.name: red-fleshed pumpkin
nodes.summary: A type of pumpkin with pale flesh and does not usually embroider, also grown are the red-fleshed, green or Spanish, Turkish bonnet or turban, and small Jerusalem artichoke pumpkins.

nodes.name: green or Spanish pumpkin
nodes.summary: The green or Spanish pumpkin is smaller, very flat and of excellent quality. It's not often grown because consumers are unaware of its qualities.

nodes.name: Turkish bonnet or turban pumpkin
nodes.summary: Turkish bonnet or turban pumpkin is a type of pumpkin that is very firm and highly prized, it has a turban shape and three or four are left on the same plant

nodes.name: Jerusalem artichoke pumpkin
nodes.summary: The Jerusalem artichoke pumpkin is a small, highly prized variety with gadrooned fruit, often leaving up to six or eight on the same plant.

nodes.name: Scorsoner
nodes.summary: Scorsoner is a biennial or trisannual plant native to Spain, belonging to the Compositae family. Its edible part is the root, while its leaves can also be eaten. Cultivation involves sowing seeds at the end of April or in May for market gardening, or in August elsewhere.

nodes.name: Spain
nodes.summary: Spain is mentioned as the native country of the Scorsonera plant.

nodes.name: PANAIS
nodes.summary: PANAIS is a plant of the Umbelliferae family, native to Paris. It has a simple taproot and edible root used in soup. Paris market gardeners produce little parsnip due to limited use in cuisine.

nodes.name: Populaceae
nodes.summary: Parsnip is a plant of the Umbelliferae family, with a simple, taproot and edible root.

nodes.name: purslane
nodes.summary: Purslane is still taking up part of the market gardener's time in August; supplies rise quickly and must be sown very often.

nodes.name: young shoots
nodes.summary: Young shoots of chives are susceptible to green aphid and require regular wetting.

nodes.name: beet
nodes.summary: Beet is a biennial plant, indigenous, with broad oval petiolate leaves and numerous small flowers, followed by kidney-shaped seeds.

nodes.name: radial leaves
nodes.summary: Radial leaves are cultivated varieties of pear and chard used in market gardening. They can be harvested by cutting the plant at a certain height or picking individual leaves by hand.

nodes.name: mild weather
nodes.summary: Refers to weather without frosts during which the bedding of cardoon plants is uncovered.

nodes.name: Carduaceae
nodes.summary: Carduaceae is a section of plants in the Compositae family and artichoke genus.

nodes.name: Cardoons de Tours
nodes.summary: Cardoons de Tours is a type of cardoon from the Compositae family, cultivated in Paris for its edible leaf stalks and ribs. It is a biennial plant, thorny, with full ribs, making it superior to the Spanish variety.

nodes.name: Spanish cardoon
nodes.summary: Spanish cardoon is a biennial plant from the south of France with a simple, taproot and edible leaf stalks and ribs.

nodes.name: leaf stalks
nodes.summary: Edible parts of the cardoon, leaf stalks are harvested for consumption.

nodes.name: ribs
nodes.summary: The ribs of the cabbage can be attacked by diseases such as canker and rot.

nodes.name: root leaves
nodes.summary: long, whitish, pinnatifid, often thorny root leaves; edible parts of the cardoon are the leaf stalks and ribs

nodes.name: watering can of water
nodes.summary: A watering can of water is used every two days for beautiful cardoons in the Paris marshes during dry and hot weather.

nodes.name: straw tie
nodes.summary: used to bind cardoon leaves together in three places

nodes.name: CELERI
nodes.summary: A plant of the Umbelliferae family, in the Aminineae section, and of the parsley genus.

nodes.name: Aminineae section
nodes.summary: A plant classification within the Umbelliferae family

nodes.name: parsley genus
nodes.summary: A plant of the Umbelliferae family, in the Aminineae section.

nodes.name: CELERI-RAVE
nodes.summary: CELERI-RAVE is a type of celery that is sown at the same time as Turkish Celery but planted once and at a greater distance. It is less costly to blanch than other varieties.

nodes.name: celery root
nodes.summary: Celery root is excellent cooked and can be cultivated by broadcasting seeds on a well-ploughed bed in late March or April.

nodes.name: CELERY FOR CUTTING
nodes.summary: CELERY FOR CUTTING is a type of celery distinguished by its smaller size and hollow ribs. It is sown in late March or April, cultivated differently and more simply than other varieties.

nodes.name: RAIPONCE
nodes.summary: Raiponce is harvested in November and December.

nodes.name: Campanulaceae
nodes.summary: Campanulaceae family

nodes.name: Campanula
nodes.summary: Campanula is a plant of the Campanulaceae family, native to Paris. It has simple, taprooted roots and radical, oval, lanceolate leaves.

nodes.name: ash
nodes.summary: Ash is used as a mixing agent and for cauterizing wounds in plants, such as onions.

nodes.name: raipouce
nodes.summary: Raipouce is a plant that has restricted cultivation in the marshes due to low profitability and competition from naturally grown raipouce being supplied to capital markets at very low prices.

nodes.name: Dryadae order
nodes.summary: A plant order that includes strawberries

nodes.name: genus strawberry
nodes.summary: Strawberry is a plant of the Rosaceae family with several species and many varieties. They are small, herbaceous perennials native to Europe and America.

nodes.name: America
nodes.summary: Native to America, one of several species and many varieties

nodes.name: trifoliate leaves
nodes.summary: Trifoliate leaves are a type of leaf structure associated with strawberries and are characterized by three leaflets.

nodes.name: white flowers
nodes.summary: White flowers are a type of plant part that appear on the strawberry plant.

nodes.name: oval or rounded fruits
nodes.summary: oval or rounded fruits

nodes.name: Alpine strawberries
nodes.summary: Alpine strawberries are cultivated in Paris; seeds extracted from perfect maturity fruit and sown on land in June after drying out.

nodes.name: runners
nodes.summary: Runners are preferred over plants grown from seed by some, and can be planted in another season; however, the described cultivation method is simplest for market gardeners.

nodes.name: stoves
nodes.summary: Stoves are used in forcing strawberry plants to provide heat during frost. They should be ventilated as needed and rearranged when necessary.

nodes.name: strawberry plants
nodes.summary: Strawberry plants are cultivated by market gardeners using a specific method described in the text. This method is considered the simplest and most practical for market gardeners.

nodes.name: TENDER TURNIP OF THE VIRTUES
nodes.summary: TENDER TURNIP OF THE VIRTUES is an oblong, white, and very early variety of turnip that can be sown at the end of June by market gardeners but they had to give up growing them because they go to seed and stop growing if sown before June

nodes.name: CHAMPIGNON
nodes.summary: A plant of the cryptogam family and agaric genus; it grows naturally in France, on lawns of dry meadows and woods, and at the foot of piles of horse manure.

nodes.name: greenhouses
nodes.summary: used for forcing strawberry plants and other crops

nodes.name: stems and branches
nodes.summary: regarded by scholars as the stems and branches of the plant

nodes.name: quarries of Paris
nodes.summary: quarries of Paris are used by mushroom growers to cultivate mushrooms at a lower cost than market gardeners

nodes.name: hay
nodes.summary: Hay is mentioned as being present in piles of horse manure that are prepared for fertilization, and should be removed along with any large pieces of litter or wood that have not been impregnated with urine.

nodes.name: mushroom spawn
nodes.summary: Mushroom spawn is used in market gardening for growing mushrooms. Old millstones can be used as a source of mushroom spawn.

nodes.name: wheel
nodes.summary: A wheel used in market gardening in Paris, specifically for a process called 'gopter', which involves coating the wheel with fine earth and solidifying it by striking with the back of a shovel.

nodes.name: filaments
nodes.summary: Filaments are a part of plant anatomy that extend into the wheel.

nodes.name: millstones
nodes.summary: Millstones are used in market gardening to grow mushrooms and other crops; they can be prepared with manure that has been vegetated and spread throughout the trench.

nodes.name: mushroom seed
nodes.summary: Mushroom seed forms after millstones have been in a certain state for 15-20 days, and mushrooms can be picked every two days, with well-grown millstones producing mushrooms for 2-3 months.

nodes.name: quarries
nodes.summary: Quarries in and around Paris are used for mushroom cultivation, producing large quantities that consume a lot of manure.

nodes.name: mole
nodes.summary: a disease affecting mushroom cultivation in cellars

nodes.name: whitewash
nodes.summary: used in spawn preparation to create new whitewash slabs

nodes.name: spawn
nodes.summary: Spawn is used in mushroom cultivation and has a vegetative state that can spread throughout manure after 20-25 days.

nodes.name: diseases
nodes.summary: Insect and disease damage to cheeses; market gardeners speak of them in non-scientific terms.

nodes.name: vegetable diseases
nodes.summary: Vegetable diseases caused by insects and other factors can be difficult for market gardeners to understand in scientific terms.

nodes.name: entomologists
nodes.summary: Entomologists study insects and their impact on vegetable growth and diseases in market gardening.

nodes.name: nosologists
nodes.summary: Nosologists are mentioned in a discussion about insect and disease damage to vegetables, indicating that their language is unfamiliar in the context of market gardening.

nodes.name: criocer
nodes.summary: A red insect that eats the bark of asparagus stalks and can be numerous enough to cover them.

nodes.name: moths
nodes.summary: Moths torment plants and also appear in lamb's lettuce causing damage when they start to make galleries; moths are the greatest enemy of melons, settling on leaves, biting epidermis, sucking parenchyma, harming vegetation.

nodes.name: White grubs
nodes.summary: White grubs torment claws in the ground.

nodes.name: Asparagus claws
nodes.summary: Asparagus claws are prone to melting or rotting in greasy and damp soils.

nodes.name: Greasy, damp soils
nodes.summary: Greasy, damp soils are not directly mentioned in the given text; however, it is mentioned that 'damping-off' is a disease affecting little wild chicory when grown on a bed and under a frame, caused by defects in the soil among other factors.

nodes.name: Kermes
nodes.summary: Kermes is a pest that can be destroyed by brushing the plant with water.

nodes.name: Black fly
nodes.summary: The black fly is an enemy of the cardoon plant. It attaches itself to the leaves in huge numbers, slowing and stopping vegetation, causing the plant to wither.

nodes.name: Carrot seedlings
nodes.summary: Carrot seedlings have a formidable enemy in dry soils, a small spider that cuts young plants as they emerge, which can be destroyed by frequently watering the young plant.

nodes.name: Dry soils
nodes.summary: Dry soils are prone to damping-off which can affect carrots

nodes.name: damping-off
