nodes.summary: Damping-off is a disease that affects carrots and curly endive to some extent in unsuitable soils, causing germinating seeds to be reversed and killed.

nodes.name: jaundice
nodes.summary: a disease that can affect spinach in summer if the weather is very hot; stopped or reduced by plenty of watering

nodes.name: curly endive
nodes.summary: Curly endive can be stored for eight or nine years and is subject to the same diseases as escarole.

nodes.name: cutworm
nodes.summary: The cutworm is also known as the earthworm or large gray caterpillar and is an enemy of curly endive.

nodes.name: gray caterpillar
nodes.summary: The gray caterpillar is also known as the cutworm or earthworm and can harm curly endive.

nodes.name: wild lettuce
nodes.summary: Wild lettuce is exposed to diseases such as damping-off and rust in various stages of cultivation.

nodes.name: cattail
nodes.summary: The cattail can harm wild chicory by making its galleries in all directions, reversing the germinating seeds and killing them.

nodes.name: damping-off disease
nodes.summary: Damping-off disease affects little wild chicory when grown on a bed and under a frame. Causes include soil defects, excessive humidity, and lack of sunlight.

nodes.name: ground lice
nodes.summary: Ground lice are enemies of cauliflowers and other cabbages that can be stopped by watering three or four times a day or shading the seedling.

nodes.name: caterpillars
nodes.summary: Caterpillars eat pear leaves and are accompanied by a small red-headed horsefly that cuts the stalk in the ground.

nodes.name: white butterfly
nodes.summary: The caterpillar of the white butterfly eats only the leaves of cauliflower and cabbages but devours quickly.

nodes.name: Canker
nodes.summary: Canker is a disease that can occur in tomatoes, melons and tarragon.

nodes.name: rot
nodes.summary: Rot is a disease that can occur in cabbage, leading to death and affecting the trunk, ribs, and pith.

nodes.name: pith
nodes.summary: The pith of a cabbage can be affected by diseases such as canker and rot, which can lead to death.

nodes.name: greyflies
nodes.summary: Greyflies attack cauliflower heads as soon as they begin to appear and would devour them if not regularly destroyed; later, green aphids besiege the shoots by the millions, sometimes rendering the seed crop useless.

nodes.name: green aphids
nodes.summary: Green aphids attack shoots of plants such as cabbage and romaine lettuce; can be destroyed by wetting the plant with a watering can and sprinkling powdered quicklime on them.

nodes.name: seed crop
nodes.summary: can be attacked by caterpillars, diseases like canker and rot, insects or greyflies, and green aphids that besiege the shoots

nodes.name: worm
nodes.summary: Causes exostoses at the base of the cabbage and lodges in its interior, hollows it out and makes it suffer.

nodes.name: guillotine or small white worm
nodes.summary: The guillotine or small white worm is a pest that eats the roots and core of cabbage, including cauliflower, and also damages onions by preventing them from growing in greasy, cool soil during wet years.

nodes.name: APPLE KALE
nodes.summary: Cabbage apples and diseases are similar to those of cauliflower

nodes.name: big green caterpillar
nodes.summary: The big green caterpillar eats spinach, and is its greatest enemy, making galleries in the spinach bed and killing a large part of it.

nodes.name: gray mold
nodes.summary: Gray mold is a fungal disease that affects beans in forced and natural cultivation, causing damage to the plant. It also affects lettuce, manifesting as coffee-colored or blackish spots, leading to plant loss.

nodes.name: Loaches
nodes.summary: Loaches eat leaves of beans in wet weather.

nodes.name: slugs
nodes.summary: Slugs eat bean and lettuce leaves in wet weather.

nodes.name: rust
nodes.summary: affects leaves, particularly on the underside, and makes them hooked.

nodes.name: CHEESE
nodes.summary: Lamb's lettuce has few enemies other than the moth

nodes.name: Miller
nodes.summary: A disease affecting lamb's lettuce when sown in damp soil or during very wet seasons, causing damage to young plants especially after watering.

nodes.name: Gray moth
nodes.summary: The gray moth is a small spider that settles on the underside of leaves, forms a small web, bites the epidermis, sucks the parenchyma, alters or destroys the sections of the leaves and harms vegetation.

nodes.name: Epidermis
nodes.summary: outermost layer of a plant's skin

nodes.name: grey melon
nodes.summary: Grey melon is susceptible to diseases such as canker and grey aphid, which causes multiplication. It's also affected by an animal that damages the leaves.

nodes.name: ulcer
nodes.summary: A disease that appears on melons as a small, livid spot on the surface of the bark, spreading rapidly and rotting the entire bark around the base if not treated promptly.

nodes.name: bark
nodes.summary: The bark of a melon plant can be affected by canker or ulcer disease, which starts as a small livid spot and spreads rapidly, rotting the entire bark around the base

nodes.name: Insects of melons
nodes.summary: Insects of melons, especially under frames, is poisoned by grey melon canker, a disease that can be cured by scraping off what is rotten and cauterizing the wound with ashes or powdered plaster.

nodes.name: disease
nodes.summary: a disease affecting melons and onions

nodes.name: tobacco
nodes.summary: used as a fumigant to destroy an insect on melons

nodes.name: flea beetle
nodes.summary: The flea beetle is an insect that can be destructive to plants, especially melons. It doesn't like water and can be destroyed by watering or fumigating with tobacco. However, this method is not yet widely accepted in market gardening.

nodes.name: powdered plaster
nodes.summary: used as a cauterizing agent for treating melon diseases

nodes.name: damping-off (nuile)
nodes.summary: damping-off (nuile) is a disease that manifests itself as a yellowish spot on the onion where it forms; it grows, the leaves turn yellow and the whole plant rots.

nodes.name: wet years
nodes.summary: wet years can cause onions to be subject to damping-off, also known as "nuile".

nodes.name: large beetle
nodes.summary: The large beetle is an insect that eats the leaves of sorrel down to the rib and drops to the ground when approached.

nodes.name: cold weather
nodes.summary: Cold weather is mentioned as a factor that causes parsley to perish from the crown and makes the caterpillar that devours sorrel leaves disappear.

nodes.name: heavy frosts
nodes.summary: Heavy frosts cause parsley to perish from the crown.

nodes.name: diseases of chervil
nodes.summary: Diseases of chervil is a disease that can affect parsley and has reddish spots on the leaves.

nodes.name: Crown
nodes.summary: Crown is subject to heavy frosts that cause it to perish from the crown.

nodes.name: Pimpernelle
nodes.summary: Pimpernelle is a salad garnish that can be attacked by melt in certain areas.

nodes.name: Melt
nodes.summary: Melt is a disease that affects purslane, causing it to perish when sown too thickly in heavy, loamy soil.

nodes.name: Mite
nodes.summary: Mite is an insect that cuts leeks between two plots of land when newly planted.

nodes.name: Horsefly
nodes.summary: The horsefly is a pest that cuts the stalk of pears and pear cacti in the ground.

nodes.name: Unseasonal weather
nodes.summary: Unseasonal weather can cause rust on pear leaves and whole plant is subject to melting.

nodes.name: Loamy soil
nodes.summary: Loamy soil is heavy and subject to melting, which also happens when it is sown too thickly in purslane.

nodes.name: giraumonts
nodes.summary: Giraumonts are a type of pumpkin that requires to be sown on beds last month and planted permanently.

nodes.name: turbans
nodes.summary: Turbans are a type of pumpkin that can be exposed to the same diseases as melons but their vigorous vegetation protects them.

nodes.name: RAVES
nodes.summary: RAVES are affected by diseases such as melting when sown on the bed, and flea beetles or flea beetles that devour them.

nodes.name: cruciferous plants
nodes.summary: Cruciferous plants are horribly tormented by flea beetles or flea beetles, which devour them, and crickets also destroy them through the roots.

nodes.name: crickets
nodes.summary: Cricket is a pest that destroys young turnip and radish seedlings through the roots. Several ways to destroy it include giving it a pile of manure to hide in, filling its hole with water and pouring oil over the water.

nodes.name: Black aphid
nodes.summary: Aphid that is as harmful as the previous one but is destroyed by heavy thunderstorms.

nodes.name: White aphid
nodes.summary: White aphid is a pest that attaches itself to the plant's root and causes it to languish; sometimes destroyed by watering.

nodes.name: Red-headed chafer
nodes.summary: Larva of the small chafer that eats and cuts the root of romaine plants, killing them; also known as the red-headed worm.

nodes.name: The miller
nodes.summary: The miller is a disease that manifests itself as white spots on the underside of leaves, causing the plant to languish.

nodes.name: White spots
nodes.summary: White spots are a disease that manifests itself as white spots on the underside of leaves; these spots multiply very quickly and cause the plant to languish.

nodes.name: Red collet
nodes.summary: A disease affecting romaine plants, characterized by a sudden cold spell after vigorous growth.

nodes.name: Melting
nodes.summary: Melting is a fatal disease that manifests itself as a black spot on the plant's collar and has no cure.

nodes.name: Illnesses
nodes.summary: Illnesses are a threat to plants in Parisian market gardens; common ones include Melting and Rust. Cricket infestations can be treated by drowning or smothering. Fly speck is another issue affecting romaine plants

nodes.name: Fly speck
nodes.summary: Fly speck is a disease that affects romaine plants. It manifests as gray or russet spots on the inner leaves, which soon turn rotten and kill the plant.

nodes.name: Scorole
nodes.summary: Scorole is a type of plant that can be affected by fly speck disease, which manifests as gray or russet spots on the inner leaves.

nodes.name: wet weather
nodes.summary: wet weather can cause canker at the foot of tomatoes

nodes.name: seed stage
nodes.summary: Seed stage refers to the early growth stage of mushrooms in earthen millstones, covered by their shirts, believed by some market gardeners to be vulnerable to the influence of rainbows.

nodes.name: earthen millstones
nodes.summary: Earthen millstones are used by market gardeners in Paris and can be affected by sudden drops in temperature when a rainbow appears.

nodes.name: lightning
nodes.summary: Lightning can kill fungus grain of mushrooms on the ground according to market gardeners in Paris.

nodes.name: dust
nodes.summary: Dust is affected by whirlwinds of wind and dust that rip up or break down trees and uncover the houses they pass through.

nodes.name: arbres
nodes.summary: Arbres can be damaged by trombes de vent, whirlwinds that rip up trees and cause damage to market gardens.

nodes.name: engrais
nodes.summary: Fertilizers used in market gardening; improving substances like water for irrigation.

nodes.name: pelle
nodes.summary: a tool used for farming and gardening, possibly referring to a shovel

nodes.name: arrosoir
nodes.summary: an equipment used by market gardeners to throw water against a whirlpool to break it.

nodes.name: eau
nodes.summary: Water for irrigation is a crucial aspect of market gardening in Paris.

nodes.name: Asperges
nodes.summary: Asperges produce round, succulent, pea-sized fruits that ripen in autumn and contain six black seeds.

nodes.name: seed envelope
nodes.summary: A small fruit, divided into two parts, each containing a seed under a tough, oval-shaped envelope, 3 millimeters long, with four lines of long lamellar teeth on the outside, making them look bristly.

nodes.name: Cardoon seed
nodes.summary: Cardoon seed is good for three to four years and requires specific cultivation methods. To obtain seeds, leave a few plants in place during autumn, cut off large leaves as frost approaches, cover them with litter, and uncover in spring for the stalk, flower, and ripen seeds by October.

nodes.name: Cherfeuil
nodes.summary: Cherfeuil seeds are good for six years.

nodes.name: powdered quicklime
nodes.summary: Used to destroy green aphid on young shoots of plants. Applied by wetting the plant and sprinkling powdered quicklime on the aphids.

nodes.name: CHOU-POMME
nodes.summary: CHOU-POMME is a plant that produces seeds used for planting. Also known as apple cabbage, it includes cabbages that form an apple with their central leaves, such as York sprouts and savoy cabbage.

nodes.name: Milan cabbages
nodes.summary: Milan cabbages are a type of cabbage that produces seeds good for eight or nine years.

nodes.name: Cress Alénais
nodes.summary: Cress Alénais is a plant that is part of the chapter on seed production and sowing in the manual of market gardening.

nodes.name: petite laitue à graine noire
nodes.summary: Petite laitue à graine noire is a type of lettuce with ellipsoid seeds that are black and retain their germinative capacity for three or four years.

nodes.name: white aigrette
nodes.summary: a white aigrette rises out of the calyx and detaches the seeds; and, as these aigrettes only appear successively

nodes.name: LETTUCEJROUGE
nodes.summary: LETTUCEJROUGE is a type of lettuce that heads in May and has black seed harvested in July.

nodes.name: ORDINARY LAMB'S LETTUCE
nodes.summary: ORDINARY LAMB'S LETTUCE is a type of lettuce that can be grown throughout the summer, but the best apples for seed are left to set in June; they are grayish-white and are harvested in September.

nodes.name: REGENCE CITRUS
nodes.summary: REGENCE CITRUS is a type of plant that requires specific conditions for seed harvesting. Its seeds are smaller in size and have a small toothed crown at the top.

nodes.name: melon seeds
nodes.summary: Melon seeds are oblong, compressed, and leathery-skinned. They can be kept for 4-5 years but may remain viable for up to 25 years.

nodes.name: cantaloupe seeds
nodes.summary: Cantaloupe seeds are oblong, compressed, yellowish-white and smooth; they are 11 millimeters long and 6 millimeters wide.

nodes.name: market melon seeds
nodes.summary: Market melon seeds are oblong, compressed, leathery-skinned, yellowish-white and smooth; they are 1 centimeter long and 5 millimeters wide and can be good for four or five years.

nodes.name: seed of sorrel
nodes.summary: The seed of sorrel is a triangular, brown, shiny, unilocular capsule, indehiscent and containing the true seed.

nodes.name: carrot seed
nodes.summary: Carrot seed is mentioned alongside parsnip seed as they belong to the same family.

nodes.name: parisnep seed
nodes.summary: The parsnip seed is treated like the carrot and grows at the same time; it belongs to the same family, but its seed is very different from that of the carrot.

nodes.name: fruit of parsley
nodes.summary: Parsley rises in spring and ripens its fruit in June; these fruits are oval, longitudinally striated, 2 millimeters long, didymous or dividing into two bean-shaped parts, each containing a seed.

nodes.name: seed of parsley
nodes.summary: Parsley seeds are oval, longitudinally striated, 2 millimeters long, didymous or dividing into two bean-shaped parts, each containing a seed, and retain their germinative capacity for four or five years.

nodes.name: seed of burnet
nodes.summary: Burnet seed is not often sown in gardens because it is a perennial plant usually propagated by bursting.

nodes.name: burnet seed
nodes.summary: Burnet seed is a perennial plant not often sown in gardens because it is usually propagated by bursting.

nodes.name: POIRÉE
nodes.summary: POIRÉE is a plant stored over winter; in the spring it comes up and ripens its seeds in summer. The seeds must be dried in the sun and beaten to detach them from the plant; these seeds are fruits as large as peas, very rough on the outside, black and hard as jet on the inside, and contain a single kernel: their germinative capacity is preserved for three or four years.

nodes.name: Jerusalem artichoke
nodes.summary: Jerusalem artichoke seeds are smaller than large pumpkin seeds, and have a bead surrounding them; they keep well for four or five years.

nodes.name: seeds of pink radishes
nodes.summary: Seeds of pink radishes are good for three years. They can be harvested like those of black radish.

nodes.name: pink radish
nodes.summary: Pink radish seeds are sown again in autumn.

nodes.name: fields
nodes.summary: fields are used for various vegetables including cardoon, celery, cauliflower, pear-cardoon, leek, sorrel and tarragon which require heavy wetting in August.

nodes.name: Roman
nodes.summary: Roman is a person associated with information on plants such as cabbage and lettuces.

nodes.name: GRAY ROMAIN
nodes.summary: Gray romaine is sown in the open ground throughout the summer and seeds can be harvested multiple times.

nodes.name: melons
nodes.summary: Melons are sold in May along with other produce such as green romaine, carrots, turnips, and radishes.

nodes.name: prickly spinach
nodes.summary: Prickly spinach is a type of spinach grown in Paris and described by J.G. Moreau and J. Daverne. It is sown after the 15th of August along with white onions, radishes, and lamb's lettuce and is sold from autumn to spring.

nodes.name: Dutch beans
nodes.summary: Dutch beans are sown and covered with frames as the first frosts approach.

nodes.name: summer cauliflower
nodes.summary: Summer cauliflower is a crop grown in September and abundantly watered. It can be planted after the melon trenches are emptied and filled with soil.

nodes.name: escarole and chicory
nodes.summary: Escarole and chicory are grown in September, they take pride of place.

nodes.name: cœur-de-bœuf cabbage
nodes.summary: cœur-de-bœuf cabbage is a type of cabbage grown in Paris in July for autumn consumption.

nodes.name: small salomon cauliflowers
nodes.summary: Small salomon cauliflowers are sown from September 8th to the 12th for transplantation later in nurseries.

nodes.name: large salomon cauliflowers
nodes.summary: Large salomon cauliflowers are sown in September, between the 8th to the 12th, and transplanted either on ados or in trays before being planted on beds or open ground when the season arrives.

nodes.name: trunks and frames
nodes.summary: Trunks and frames are prepared in August and used for irrigation and gardening purposes; they're often put up during the night by candlelight or in the morning before starting work.

nodes.name: chicory from Meaux
nodes.summary: Chicory from Meaux is harvested in the month of October along with other vegetables like escarole and cauliflower.

nodes.name: raiponium
nodes.summary: Raiponium can be sown in October.

nodes.name: early green romaine
nodes.summary: Early green romaine is a type of lettuce that can be sown in the first days of October for bedding or coastal planting.

nodes.name: oxheart cabbage
nodes.summary: Oxheart cabbage is a type of cabbage mentioned in the context of market gardening in Paris, specifically in September when it is transplanted into the nursery.

nodes.name: red lettuces
nodes.summary: Red lettuces are available in the marshes in summer and fall.

nodes.name: gray lettuces
nodes.summary: Gray lettuces are sown in August under cloche and transplanted onto teens, also available in September.

nodes.name: Raipons
nodes.summary: Raipons are available in February, along with plants such as chicory, escarole, cauliflower.

nodes.name: supports
nodes.summary: Supports are used around beds and plants to prevent frost from penetrating

nodes.name: cauliflower transplants
nodes.summary: Cauliflower transplants are protected from frost by covering with litter, and some may be transplanted onto a frame for the same reason as romaine.

nodes.name: celeriac
nodes.summary: Celeriac is harvested in November, along with black lettuce, lamb's lettuce, leek; celery and celeriac can be kept in the cellar by the hub.

nodes.name: fumier sec
nodes.summary: Fumier sec (dry manure) is used between the bells of ados to protect plants from frost and in paths during winter months.

nodes.name: asparagus blanc
nodes.summary: Asparagus blanc is mentioned in the text as a plant that needs to be heated during winter.

nodes.name: carottes
nodes.summary: Carrots are mentioned in December as being sown early in the garden.

nodes.name: radis
nodes.summary: Radis is mentioned as a crop in December that needs extra care to preserve due to the cold weather.

nodes.name: carder-pear
nodes.summary: Carder-pear is a type of plant that requires specific care during winter. It should be covered with litter and uncovered as soon as it thaws to protect it from frost.

nodes.name: snow peas
nodes.summary: Snow peas can be sown in cold frames under snow to yield early crops; they can also be prepared as mushroom wheels for a March harvest.

nodes.name: early peas
nodes.summary: Early peas can be sown in this month for those who did not sow snow peas under cold frames in the previous month.

nodes.name: cold frames
nodes.summary: used for sowing snow peas or early peas and preparing mushroom wheels; protects young plants from frost

nodes.name: mushroom wheels
nodes.summary: Mushroom wheels can be prepared in January to yield in March.

nodes.name: loose cauliflower
nodes.summary: Loose cauliflower is available for sale in January and can be protected from frost using cold frames.

nodes.name: crepe
nodes.summary: Used to make new layers to plant black lettuce under frame

nodes.name: caroltes hátivest
nodes.summary: Caroltes hátivest is a type of plant that grows under frames and can be sown in layers with other plants like lettuces and romaine.

nodes.name: small wild chiconée
nodes.summary: Small wild chiconée is a type of plant that grows under frames and requires good aggots to cover new layers.

nodes.name: meuf manure
nodes.summary: Meuf manure is used to line paths.

nodes.name: periwinkle
nodes.summary: Periwinkle is harvested in November along with other plants like sorrel, parsley, and chervil.

nodes.name: fine chicory
nodes.summary: Fine chicory is sown in February on beds and under chassis to be harvested before that sown in the open ground.

nodes.name: flax beans
nodes.summary: Flax beans are used for transplanting on other layers; they can be sown in the open ground at the same time as romaine and cauliflowers.

nodes.name: bean sprouts
nodes.summary: Beans are planted on beds and covered with trays.

nodes.name: green beans
nodes.summary: Green beans are available in May and June, and a market garden must always have them ready to replace those that have reached maturity. They continue to be sown along with other winter crops including chicory, escarole, turnips, radishes, chervil, maidenhair

nodes.name: preserved cauliflower
nodes.summary: Available for sale in March and planted in February in the open ground many cauliflowers raised under chassis.

nodes.name: new lettuce for cutting
nodes.summary: New lettuce for cutting available in March

nodes.name: chicories
nodes.summary: Chicories are sown in March in the open ground in our marshes and strong, cold soils, including fine and half-fine chicories.

nodes.name: Alençon cress
nodes.summary: Alençon cress is a type of plant that can be sown in March, specifically in strong, cold soils.

nodes.name: new radishes
nodes.summary: New radishes are harvested in February along with black lettuce and green romaine.

nodes.name: new carrots
nodes.summary: New carrots are available in February and are listed alongside other market gardening products such as black lettuce, new radishes, green romaine, sorrel, parsley, chervil, lamb's lettuce, raipons, green beans, leeks, spinach, small wild chicory, champignon, tarragon, white and green asparagus, preserved cauliflower.

nodes.name: champiguon
nodes.summary: Champiguon is a type of plant that can be harvested in February.

nodes.name: cucurbits
nodes.summary: Cucurbits include various pumpkins, giraumonts, cucumbers and gherkins. They are planted in the open ground in spring, summer and autumn.

nodes.name: petit prescott fond blanc
nodes.summary: a type of melon sold in May

nodes.name: certeuil
nodes.summary: Certeuil is a type of plant sold by market gardeners in Paris during the month of May.

nodes.name: salad burnet
nodes.summary: Sold in May and possibly July, salad burnet is a plant that requires market gardeners to sow in such a way that they always have plants ready to replace those that have reached maturity.

nodes.name: carrot beds
nodes.summary: Carrot beds are mentioned as being turned over in August to plant melons.

nodes.name: Sugar loaf
nodes.summary: Sugar loaf is a type of cabbage, specifically 'hâtif en pain de sucre'.

nodes.name: Beef hearts
nodes.summary: Beef hearts are mentioned as a sale item in June and September alongside other produce like lettuces, cauliflower, melon, cucumber, chicory, York sprouts, sugar loaf.

nodes.name: pear chard
nodes.summary: Pear chard is a type of vegetable that can be sown in June, along with lettuces and cardoons.

nodes.name: lettuces of the season
nodes.summary: Lettuces of the season are planted every ten or twelve days in June for consumption during that time.

nodes.name: celery turcet
nodes.summary: Celery turcet is a type of celery that starts being transplanted in the winter.

nodes.name: celery raye
nodes.summary: Celery raye is a type of celery that is typically transplanted in the winter season.

nodes.name: lady's mantle
nodes.summary: Lady's mantle is a plant that does not mind the sun and must be sown very often during certain seasons.

nodes.name: painrde-sucre cabbage
nodes.summary: Painrde-sucre cabbage is a type of cabbage mentioned as being grown in Paris market gardens, particularly in the month of August.

nodes.name: ton ate
nodes.summary: Tomate is a produce item mentioned for July

nodes.name: cerfenil
nodes.summary: Cerfenil is a type of plant grown by market gardeners in Paris, typically planted in July.

nodes.name: chive
nodes.summary: Chives are a produce item mentioned for August and can be grown by market gardeners in Paris.

nodes.name: kale
nodes.summary: Kale is planted in July for winter consumption.

nodes.name: maidenhair
nodes.summary: Maidenhair is sown in July, along with other crops like celery, cauliflowers, kale, Brussels sprouts, red cabbage, chicory, escarole, turnips, radishes, and chervil.

nodes.name: fine chicories
nodes.summary: Fine chicories are seasonal vegetables available in September, along with other produce like melons, cucumbers, and lettuces.

nodes.name: semi-fine chicories
nodes.summary: A type of chicory grown in Paris, mentioned as part of autumn plantings in the trenches after melon harvest.

nodes.name: sweetbread cabbage
nodes.summary: sweetbread cabbage is mentioned as one of the seasonal vegetables in September and October

nodes.name: cut celery
nodes.summary: Cut celery is a seasonal vegetable available in September, often watered abundantly.

nodes.name: belle-dame
nodes.summary: Belle-dame is a seasonal vegetable available in the marshes of Paris during the last month of the horticultural year.

nodes.name: cauliflower apples
nodes.summary: Cauliflower apples were discussed in terms of their preservation and cultivation, particularly during the winter season.

links.name: ContainsOrHasPart

links.name: AssociatedWith

links.name: USES

links.name: RELATES_TO

links.name: LocatedInOrAt

links.name: AssociatedWith
links.fact: The Société royale et centrale d'agriculture encouraged and motivated the authors to join the competition.

links.name: RELATES_TO

links.name: AssociatedWith
links.fact: The Société royale et centrale d'agriculture de la Seine has always taken a great interest in market gardening in Paris.

links.name: ContainsOrHasPart
links.fact: The market gardening class is part of the market gardeners group.

links.name: LocatedInOrAt
links.fact: Market gardening is typically done in Paris.

links.name: RELATES_TO
links.fact: Market gardening in Paris has never been perfected except by practice.

links.name: AssociatedWith
links.fact: Market gardening is associated with the Royal and Central Agricultural Society.

links.name: DiffersFrom
